The essays by Miller and Straus give complete bibliographic references on first mention in their notes. I have maintained this apparatus here and followed it in my own essays. Bethea's essay uses shorthand references to the "Works Cited" section in his chapter (excluding references to well-known literary works). Because each essay has its own set of references, there is inevitably overlap between the individual studies and the final Select Bibliography. At the same time, the individual contributions contain some references that do not appear in the Select Bibliography, which lists only studies treating The Idiot. At the same time, the individual contributions contain some references that do not appear in the Select Bibliography, which lists only studies treating The Idiot. All references to Dostoevsky's works and letters in this volume are to the Polnoe sobranie sochinenii v tridsati tomakh, ed. V. G. Bazanov et al. (Leningrad: Nauka, 1972-90). The reference "8:123," for example, refers to page 123 of volume 8. Volumes 28-30, which contain Dostoevsky's letters, are each divided into two books. Thus, "28.2:136" refers to page 136 of book 2 of volume 28. The dates of Dostoevsky's letters are also included in the citations. It might help to recall that the Russian calendar at this point lagged behind the Western calendar by twelve days. When two dates are given (for example, July 9/21), the first is the Russian date, the second a translation of that date into the Western calendar. The Select Bibliography uses the Library of Congress transliteration system. My contributions use a more popular mode of rendering Russian names. In the reprinted essays, the usage of the author has been maintained. Translations of letters included in the Primary Sources are those of David Lowe. Translations of other material in the Primary Sources are my own. I INTRODUCTION Introduction to The Idiot Part 1 : Where, When, and How The Idiot Was Written LIZA KNAPP 1. Escape from Petersburg On Holy Friday 1867, Fyodor Dostoevsky and his young wife, Anna, left Russia for what they thought would be a few months. They did not return until July 1871. In setting off for Europe, Dostoevsky left behind him in Saint Petersburg heavy debts and complicated family obligations. Dostoevsky's first wife, Marya Dmitrievna, had died of consumption in April 1864, leaving Dostoevsky in charge of her teenage son (by her first husband) Pavel (Pasha) Isaev (b. 1848). Dostoevsky's beloved brother Mikhail had died a few months later, in June 1864. At this point, Dostoevsky took on responsibility for his brother's family. Out of loyalty, he also undertook to pay back debts his brother left behind. Dostoevsky had met Anna Grigoryevna Snitkina on October 4/16, 1866, when she began to serve as his stenographer. He needed to finish the manuscript of The Gambler by the end of October; otherwise, he would have had to forfeit the rights to (and profit from) all his works for the next nine years. (So desperate had Dostoevsky been for money that, for the sake of an advance, he had agreed to these conditions, set by the infamous publisher Stellovsky.) Dostoevsky frantically sought some way of meeting the deadline. At the suggestion of a friend, he decided to try working with a stenographer. To Dostoevsky's great relief, this new method of composition by dictation worked efficiently. He spent his birthday (October 30) making final corrections to the manuscript he had finished dictating ---

otherwise, he would have had to forfeit the rights to (and profit from) all his works for the next nine years. (So desperate had Dostoevsky been for money that, for the sake of an advance, he had agreed to these conditions, set by the infamous publisher Stellovsky.) Dostoevsky frantically sought some way of meeting the deadline. At the suggestion of a friend, he decided to try working with a stenographer. To Dostoevsky's great relief, this new method of composition by dictation worked efficiently. He spent his birthday (October 30) making final corrections to the manuscript he had finished dictating --- Page 3 --- the day before and delivered the manuscript the next day. Dostoevsky then invited Anna Grigoryevna to help him finish Crime and Punishment. Soon thereafter, he proposed to her. They were married in February 1867. By all accounts their marriage developed into a happy one and it provided Dostoevsky with the stable family life he had longed for. Initially, however, the new couple found their life in Saint Petersburg trying, or rather, Anna Grigoryevna especially did. Dostoevsky's creditors were hounding them and debtors' prison threatened. Dostoevsky's extended family continued to make demands on him. They did not cotton to his new wife and they behaved toward her in ways which Anna Grigoryevna found distressing and demeaning, but which her husband seemed not to notice. Hoping to save the marriage by removing herself and her husband from the trying situation in Saint Petersburg, Anna pushed for the trip abroad. She pawned her dowry to raise the money necessary for them to get away. Of course, Dostoevsky's Saint Petersburg relatives' requests for money and Dostoevsky's concern about them never ceased. But now transactions with them took place by mail. Dostoevsky and his wife were left very much on their own together for the whole period of their exile, and they developed a strong sense of mutual dependence and love. They had little social contact except for occasional encounters with old Russian acquaintances who were also abroad and a few visits from Anna's mother and brother. They kept in touch with friends and family by letter. Dostoevsky followed current events in Russia, to the extent possible, by reading Russian periodicals available in the West and by asking his correspondents, especially Apollon Maikov, for news. The Dostoevskys moved from Germany (Dresden, Baden -Baden) to Switzerland (Basel, Geneva, Vevey), to Italy (Milan, Florence) during the first two years and then found themselves staying in Dresden until their return to Russia. All along the way, they expressed their longing to go home, and yet financial, literary, and familial obstacles of one sort or another seemed to conspire to keep them in Europe. 2. Gambling and Epilepsy The time Dostoevsky and his wife spent abroad, especially in the early stages, was punctuated by attacks of Dostoevsky's gambling and epilepsy, both of which caused great suffering and hardship to the couple. As on his previous trips abroad, Dostoevsky could not resist the roulette table. Oppressed by

during the first two years and then found themselves staying in Dresden until their return to Russia. All along the way, they expressed their longing to go home, and yet financial, literary, and familial obstacles of one sort or another seemed to conspire to keep them in Europe. 2. Gambling and Epilepsy The time Dostoevsky and his wife spent abroad, especially in the early stages, was punctuated by attacks of Dostoevsky's gambling and epilepsy, both of which caused great suffering and hardship to the couple. As on his previous trips abroad, Dostoevsky could not resist the roulette table. Oppressed by financial need and dreading working on his new novel ( The Idiot), Dostoevsky would make "one last attempt" after another to gamble their way out of their financial troubles. In the letters to his wife that he wrote when he would leave her and go off gambling, Dostoevsky would swear to reform; he would heap praise on her for being angelically forbearing and abuse on himself for being unworthy. Dostoevsky would also give a thaler -by-thaler account of the trip. He would describe his foolproof "system" for winning. Then would come the pleas to send him train fare back to her. In the aftermath of his gambling, Dostoevsky became even more desperate for money. He was constantly asking his friends, and even the occasional enemy, for loans, although he was ashamed to tell them that he had gambled away his last money. The hocking and redeeming of the Dostoevskys' few possessions was a periodic event. Eventually, Dostoevsky found that the only hope for their future was to buckle down and write the novel, which had been promised to Katkov for his journal the Russian Messenger; the advances and payments were what the Dostoevskys lived on. Sigmund Freud considered Dostoevsky's gambling to be a manifestation of his innate pathological desire for self -punishment. 1 Dostoevsky's wife regarded his gambling as "a disease" for which she feared there was no cure. Eventually Dostoevsky cured himself: he gambled for what was to be the last time in Wiesbaden on April 16/28, 1871.2 He signs the letter written to his wife that night "Yours entirely from this day on, Fyodor Dostoevsky" (emphasis added). 3 During their life abroad, Dostoevsky's epileptic attacks, from which he suffered until the end of his life, continued, although by some accounts less frequently than in the period prior to their departure. (Part of the rationale for their trip abroad had been hope that removal from Saint Petersburg would be beneficial to Dostoevsky's health.) As James Rice's discussion of the frequency of Dostoevsky's attacks reveals, 4 what Dostoevsky would mention in letters did not always coincide with the recorded facts. (Dostoevsky kept a log of his attacks and his wife's diary refers to them.) Dostoevsky passed epilepsy on to the hero of The Idiot. The attack Myshkin suffers in part 2 is generally thought to be clinically realistic and to match what Dostoevsky himself experienced. Many critics have noted the

their departure. (Part of the rationale for their trip abroad had been hope that removal from Saint Petersburg would be beneficial to Dostoevsky's health.) As James Rice's discussion of the frequency of Dostoevsky's attacks reveals, 4 what Dostoevsky would mention in letters did not always coincide with the recorded facts. (Dostoevsky kept a log of his attacks and his wife's diary refers to them.) Dostoevsky passed epilepsy on to the hero of The Idiot. The attack Myshkin suffers in part 2 is generally thought to be clinically realistic and to match what Dostoevsky himself experienced. Many critics have noted the important ways in which epilepsy has been incorporated into the novel, both its thematics and its poetics. The thematic aspect relates to the suspension of time. 5 According to Myshkin, during an epileptic fit the epileptic undergoes an experience of "there being time no more"; the experience is described in language associated with the apocalypse in the Revelation of Saint John, one of Dostoevsky's favorite texts. The epileptic, feeling released from the laws ordinarily binding him on earth, experiences a "synthesis," which for Dostoevsky was a taste of heaven. As critics have noticed, epilepsy, known as the holy disease, may have been suffered by Jesus. Although one can only guess at how epilepsy influenced Dostoevsky's poetics, it may be that Dostoevsky developed his particular brand of realism, with its "fantastic" quality, in response to epilepsy. Surely, Dostoevsky had some affinity for this kind of sensation and was drawn to it in his writing. While the "aura" during an attack was sometimes beatific, Dostoevsky suffered greatly as a result of this disease. Anna Grigoryevna describes the constant dread of these attacks, their onset, the physical torment, and the effects that lasted for days: weakness, disorientation, debilitation, gloom, and grouchiness. Especially poignant are her descriptions of the fear of death that these attacks brought on. After an attack Dostoevsky would be afraid of dying and not want to fall asleep unless she was at his side. Dostoevsky himself repeatedly associates an almost mystical sadness with his epilepsy. 6 Although Myshkin's epilepsy and the "mode of being" associated with it in the novel are often thought of as beatific, one should keep in mind that while epilepsy may have given Dostoevsky a taste of heaven, it did not do so without making parts of his life on earth hell. 3. An Encounter with Turgenev: The Telescope and Madame Bovary Before he began to work in earnest on the novel, Dostoevsky paid a visit to IvanTurgenev(i8i8 -83) in Baden-Baden in July 1867.7 Since the 1840s, relations between the two authors had undergone ups and downs. After a period of unfriendliness, the two authors had had a rapprochement in the 1860s when Dostoevsky expressed his appreciation for Turgenev's Fathers and Sons and Turgenev agreed to publish a piece in Dostoevsky's journal. But, in August 1865, a desperate Dostoevsky, gambling furiously, had borrowed money from Turgenev (he asked for one hundred thalers; Turgenev gave him fifty).

Turgenev: The Telescope and Madame Bovary Before he began to work in earnest on the novel, Dostoevsky paid a visit to IvanTurgenev(i8i8 -83) in Baden-Baden in July 1867.7 Since the 1840s, relations between the two authors had undergone ups and downs. After a period of unfriendliness, the two authors had had a rapprochement in the 1860s when Dostoevsky expressed his appreciation for Turgenev's Fathers and Sons and Turgenev agreed to publish a piece in Dostoevsky's journal. But, in August 1865, a desperate Dostoevsky, gambling furiously, had borrowed money from Turgenev (he asked for one hundred thalers; Turgenev gave him fifty). Dostoevsky had promised to pay him back soon, but never had. After he arrived in Baden-Baden in 1867, Dostoevsky had been dodging Turgenev. When Ivan Goncharov, whom Dostoevsky had met at a gaming hall, mentioned that Turgenev had caught sight of him, Dostoevsky decided that he could avoid Turgenev no longer. With his unpaid debt eating away at him, Dostoevsky felt it would be cowardly not to go and see Turgenev. (For Dostoevsky's lively description of his visit, see his letter to Maikov of August 16/28, 1867, given in Primary Sources). Everything about Turgenev, from his way of offering his cheek to be kissed to his politics, bothered Dostoevsky. As his letter to Maikov reveals, Dostoevsky used this particular encounter with Turgenev to formulate, a bit histrionically, some of his most heartfelt ideas about the whole tradition of Russian Westernizing liberals, the father of whom for Dostoevsky was the influential --- Page 4 --- literary critic Vissarion Belinsky (1811-48). Belinsky, in fact, was the subject of an article Dostoevsky was working on at that time. Belinsky was associated in Dostoevsky's mind with Turgenev: Turgenev and Dostoevsky had been young writers championed during the same period by Belinsky. Dostoevsky's relations with Belinsky soured before Belinsky's death (Belinsky accused Dostoevsky of squandering his talent). Turgenev, however, had remained on good terms with him and had even dedicated his Fathers and Sons to the memory of Belinsky. As his letter to Maikov shows, Dostoevsky disliked the way "all these trashy little liberals and progressives, primarily still of Be -linsky's school, find their greatest pleasure and satisfaction in criticizing Russia."8 Dostoevsky also objected to how the liberals boast of their atheism and "spit on" what Dostoevsky terms "the loftiest divine beauty": Christ. Here Dostoevsky continues the debate over Christ that he had begun with Belinsky in the 1840s. In fact, The Idiot may be seen as Dostoevsky's ultimate (or penultimate) answer to what he saw as the liberals' tendency to spit on Christ. 9 Dostoevsky would go on to lampoon Turgenev in his depiction of the "famous author" Karmazinov in The Devils, the novel Dostoevsky wrote after The Idiot. Within The Idiot, Dostoevsky also takes a shot at Turgenev by blaming him for the nihilist movement. But this encounter with Turgenev may have had still more profound influence on the novel: During their visit, each author offered the other some advice. Dostoevsky (as he

Belinsky in the 1840s. In fact, The Idiot may be seen as Dostoevsky's ultimate (or penultimate) answer to what he saw as the liberals' tendency to spit on Christ. 9 Dostoevsky would go on to lampoon Turgenev in his depiction of the "famous author" Karmazinov in The Devils, the novel Dostoevsky wrote after The Idiot. Within The Idiot, Dostoevsky also takes a shot at Turgenev by blaming him for the nihilist movement. But this encounter with Turgenev may have had still more profound influence on the novel: During their visit, each author offered the other some advice. Dostoevsky (as he proudly related) told Turgenev to go get a telescope, the better to see Russia with. (In Dostoevsky's view, the expatriate Turgenev was out of touch with the Russia he wrote about.) Dostoevsky may well have envisioned The Idiot as just what people like Turgenev needed in order to see what Russia was truly like. Turgenev's advice to Dostoevsky was to read Madame Bovary, which, according to Anna Grigoryevna's diary report, Turgenev billed as the best thing written anywhere in the last ten years. Turgenev presumably did not heed Dostoevsky's advice to get a telescope, but Dostoevsky heeded Turgenev's. He and his wife bought the book four days later. (The fact that Dostoevsky followed Turgenev's advice attests to his respect for Turgenev's literary instincts. This respect, as Joseph Frank has pointed out, survived all their other disagreements.) Dostoevsky signals the importance of Madame Bovary in The Idiot, when we find that this was Nastasya Filippovna's reading during her last days. 10 Thus, the encounter with Turgenev - an encounter that Dostoevsky had hoped to avoid - may have played a role in what The Idiot became. In reaction to Turgenev and all he represented to Dostoevsky, Dostoevsky wanted to make his novel as "Russian" as possible (to make it into the telescope to see Russia that Turgenev so sorely needed). And, thanks to Turgenev's tip, Madame Bovary acquired in The Idiot a Russian cousin, predating its more famous Russian cousin, Anna Karenina. (I follow here Osip Mandelshtam's use of the term "cousin" to describe the relation between Anna Karenina and Emma Bovary.) 4. A Dedication to Sonya Ivanova Dostoevsky had decided even before the novel was written that he would dedicate it to his niece Sonya Ivanova. She was the twenty -one-year-old daughter of his sister Vera and her husband A. P. Ivanov. During the last months of his first wife's life, Dostoevsky had brought her to Moscow so that Ivanov, a doctor, could care for her. During this period, Dostoevsky got to know Sonya, whose childhood he had spent in exile. Writing in 1866 to Anna Korvin -Krukovskaya (whom Dostoevsky had once courted and who is thought to have inspired the character of Aglaya Epanchin in The Idiot), Dostoevsky praised his niece, saying, "What a marvelous, bright, profound, and sincere soul." 11 His devotion to Sonya was such that not only did he dedicate the book to her, but he

P. Ivanov. During the last months of his first wife's life, Dostoevsky had brought her to Moscow so that Ivanov, a doctor, could care for her. During this period, Dostoevsky got to know Sonya, whose childhood he had spent in exile. Writing in 1866 to Anna Korvin -Krukovskaya (whom Dostoevsky had once courted and who is thought to have inspired the character of Aglaya Epanchin in The Idiot), Dostoevsky praised his niece, saying, "What a marvelous, bright, profound, and sincere soul." 11 His devotion to Sonya was such that not only did he dedicate the book to her, but he had early on persuaded his wife that if the child they awaited was a girl, she should be named after Sonya. 12 Dostoevsky corresponded with Sonya while he was abroad, somewhat sporadically but enthusiastically. His letters contain news of his life abroad and of the novel dedicated to her. One of these letters contains his claim (now famous) that in this novel he was attempting to depict a "positively beautiful man," the likes of which had not yet been put to paper, Christ having been the only model thus far. 13 With his niece, Dostoevsky tried on the role he was later to adopt with many of the readers who turned to him for advice during the publication of Diary of a Writer. Dostoevsky's letters to his niece are particularly valuable for the insights they give into his attitude toward the choices which she, as a young woman, faced in her life. Sonya Ivanova had been under pressure from her family to marry, for their convenience rather than for love. She turned to her uncle for moral support after refusing. Dostoevsky fully supported her decision not to submit to this marriage. Dostoevsky encouraged her, instead of marrying a man she did not love, to find a profession, to study. He cites the example of Nadezhda Suslova (the sister of his former lover, Polina Suslova), who had just completed her studies in Zurich to become a doctor of medicine. In a comment interesting given the importance of the "woman question" to The Idiot, Dostoevsky tells his niece: "You should know that the woman question, and especially that of the Russian woman, will definitely make several great and wonderful strides even within your lifetime."14 Although Dostoevsky distanced himself from "nihilists," he nevertheless embraced the idea of women being educated and being able to earn money in a non -demeaning way. Without these opportunities, as Dostoevsky showed in Crime and Punishment, women may feel they are compelled to sell themselves to men, whether as prostitutes (Sonya Marmeladov) or as wives of men they do not love (Dunya Raskolnikov). Perhaps his own wife was a positive model of what Dostoevsky envisioned. She had pursued her training as a stenographer with great energy and determination. Forced to interrupt her course of study when her father died, Anna Grigoryevna later managed to finish learning stenography and make a living. While she and Dostoevsky were abroad awaiting

to earn money in a non -demeaning way. Without these opportunities, as Dostoevsky showed in Crime and Punishment, women may feel they are compelled to sell themselves to men, whether as prostitutes (Sonya Marmeladov) or as wives of men they do not love (Dunya Raskolnikov). Perhaps his own wife was a positive model of what Dostoevsky envisioned. She had pursued her training as a stenographer with great energy and determination. Forced to interrupt her course of study when her father died, Anna Grigoryevna later managed to finish learning stenography and make a living. While she and Dostoevsky were abroad awaiting their first child, Anna Grigoryevna (no doubt frustrated by her husband's handling of their finances) records in her diary her hope of getting a job as stenographer for the newly reformed court system on their return to Russia: "If there were a shortage of men, they might hire a woman." And the pregnant Anna mused about how good it would be to have the money; her earnings would be "a dowry for Sonya" or something for Misha (the names they had in mind for the baby that was to be born). 15 Dostoevsky and his wife suggested to Sonya Ivanova that she consider stenography as a profession and invited her to come stay with them in Saint Petersburg on their return and study with Anna Grigoryevna's former teacher. It was crucial to Dostoevsky that his niece be able to earn a living. Dostoevsky adamantly wanted to "save" Sonya from the violation or "the swinishness" of a loveless marriage. 16 Dostoevsky's ruminations on his niece's fate, on the choices she faced, on the parental pressure to marry and her own resistance, are reflected in the novel he dedicated to her. To marry or not to marry is one of the major issues debated, in one way or another, throughout the novel. As has been suggested by Robin Feuer Miller, 17 the presentation early in the novel of the "three marriageable maidens" (the Epanchin daughters) evokes something of the aura of a Jane Austen novel, but with something definitely "askew." And by the end of The Idiot, readers find that Dostoevsky is a far cry from Jane Austen. Initially, the novel seems to promise something positive for its women: the Epanchin daughters have been allowed to eat, paint, and read to their heart's content; they have not, at least until the recent plan for Alexandra to marry Totsky, been under pressure to marry. Even Nastasya Filippovna, orphaned and seduced at a young age, appears ready for the first time in her life to take charge of her fate and belong to nobody but herself. 5. As Jacques Catteau has noted, Dostoevsky's complaints, especially as reported by his wife in her Reminiscences, have led many to the conclusion that, with more money and

plan for Alexandra to marry Totsky, been under pressure to marry. Even Nastasya Filippovna, orphaned and seduced at a young age, appears ready for the first time in her life to take charge of her fate and belong to nobody but herself. 5. From Notebooks to Part 1 At various points during his career, Dostoevsky declared his envy of writers like Tolstoy and Turgenev who had the luxury of working and reworking drafts. As Jacques Catteau has noted, Dostoevsky's complaints, especially as reported by his wife in her Reminiscences, have led many to the conclusion that, with more money and more time, Dostoevsky would have written "better" novels, meaning novels with a more conventional "aesthetic standard." On the other hand, as Catteau points out, Dostoevsky, whether he and others liked it or not, was writing in a new and innovative form, "enthralled and passionate" in Dostoevsky's own words. According to Catteau, Dostoevsky was "extremely sensitive to criticism" and did not quite dare to defend his new poetics openly. Thus he may himself have contributed to the tendency to regard his works as suffering from a lack of proper revision. 18 --- Page 5 --- Dostoevsky divided the labor of writing a novel between the "poet" and the "artist." Dostoevsky's "poet" thought of the idea or summoned up from his heartfelt experience impressions and then his "artist" gave form to this raw material (which Dostoevsky likened to a gem, with the artist then functioning as jeweler). 19 In the early stages of writing a novel, Dostoevsky would make notes for himself about ideas, characters, situations, working and reworking them, almost as if they were puzzles. (At one point, Dostoevsky wrote "Eureka!" on finding a solution to a compositional problem.) Eventually he would begin to dictate the novel in fairly finished form to his wife, who would take it down in stenography and then transcribe what he had dictated. He would then make revisions (probably minimal) and she would make a clean copy. Dostoevsky increasingly grew to depend on this method of writing. Dostoevsky began writing notes for The Idiot in earnest a few days after completing his article on Belinsky on September 14. It would take Dostoevsky three months to come up with the plan he stuck with. During this period, he made various notes about characters and situations that bear little direct resemblance to what he ended up with. He scrapped plan after plan and reworked, in various arrangements, the relations between the various characters and also the natures of his characters. 20 Notable is the fact that the Idiot, when he first appears in the notebooks, is far from being the "positively beautiful man" that Dostoevsky sought to depict in The Idiot as we know it: the original Idiot is mean, spiteful, and proud. The notebooks also show Dostoevsky trying to get the family groupings right and figuring out where the Idiot should stand in relation to the question of family. Dostoevsky proposes various scenarios, debating about whether his

scrapped plan after plan and reworked, in various arrangements, the relations between the various characters and also the natures of his characters. 20 Notable is the fact that the Idiot, when he first appears in the notebooks, is far from being the "positively beautiful man" that Dostoevsky sought to depict in The Idiot as we know it: the original Idiot is mean, spiteful, and proud. The notebooks also show Dostoevsky trying to get the family groupings right and figuring out where the Idiot should stand in relation to the question of family. Dostoevsky proposes various scenarios, debating about whether his hero should be "illegitimate" or "legitimate" but without a mother. Either way, the Idiot was to be something of an outsider, who, in the novel, seeks to clarify his position in regard to family. The notebooks also show that Dostoevsky envisioned his hero as a "sphinx" and, later, as "Christ." The general plan for The Idiot was that Dostoevsky was to send off monthly installments. What we know as part 1 of the novel appeared in two successive months, for example. Dostoevsky, like other nineteenth -century Russian writers, wrote for serial publication in the so -called thick journals. (Intended to serve as the main cultural vehicle for all Russian culture, thick journals published material of all sorts - fiction, poetry, memoirs, science, politics, philosophy. The Dostoevsky brothers themselves had published two such journals.) This arrangement meant that Dostoevsky had to write in chunks that would be effective in that format, and end the installment for a given month in a way that would keep the readers coming back for more. Further, because of his particularly desperate finances, Dostoevsky hoped that the serial publication of The Idiot would be quickly followed by a separate edition and felt himself under extra pressure to make the novel good enough to convince a publisher that a separate edition would sell. 6. Part 1 of The Idiot and December 22, 1849 The very title of the novel defines Myshkin as an idiot, although Dostoevsky kept what was to be understood by this term a mystery, to be unraveled, as Robin Feuer Miller suggests, by each reader. In the opening scenes of the novel, all who meet him find something odd about him: his lack of experience with women, his populism, his preoccupation with death, and so forth. He is identified as a "holy fool" by Rogozhin early in the novel, and by Dostoevsky himself in his notebooks to the novel. 21 The figure of the holy fool goes back to Saint Paul's Epistle to the Corinthians (1 Cor. 4:10: "We are fools for Christ's sake, but you are wise in Christ"). The holy fool is associated with saintliness, a lack of worldliness (or otherworldliness), marginality to society. And, because the notion derives from Saint Paul (who also establishes condemnation to death as a metaphor for human mortality in general), the holy fool is associated with death and, specifically, with condemnation to death. The fool is

Rogozhin early in the novel, and by Dostoevsky himself in his notebooks to the novel. 21 The figure of the holy fool goes back to Saint Paul's Epistle to the Corinthians (1 Cor. 4:10: "We are fools for Christ's sake, but you are wise in Christ"). The holy fool is associated with saintliness, a lack of worldliness (or otherworldliness), marginality to society. And, because the notion derives from Saint Paul (who also establishes condemnation to death as a metaphor for human mortality in general), the holy fool is associated with death and, specifically, with condemnation to death. The fool is marginal to this life in more than one way. The tradition of the holy fool is a rich one in Russia. One of Dostoevsky's immediate literary sources could be Grisha in Leo Tolstoy's Childhood. Grisha, a pilgrim who visits the Irtenev household, functions as a memento mori; he challenges the status quo, the social structure, and ultimately all systems, including language. Further, Grisha rejects the notion that one human being should judge and punish another: he voices his opinion that there is no need for Irtenev, the narrator's father, to punish his serf for failing to control one of the dogs. Grisha speaks in a manner that is incomprehensible to the narrator's father (who voices his opinion that holy fools should all be locked up) but is comprehensible to his mother (who welcomes holy fools). Much as Tolstoy's narrative creates a rift between those who reject and those who embrace holy fools, with the child narrator left to choose sides, Dostoevsky forces all who encounter his idiot, the heroes and heroines within the plot and the reader of the text, to respond to him in some way, and in so doing, take a stand on ultimate questions of life and death. Dostoevsky's readers have always been quick to see in Myshkin certain autobiographical elements. 22 Additionally, it has been noted that Dostoevsky seems to make a cameo appearance in the novel as Myshkin's acquaintance in the insane asylum who was condemned to death and reprieved at the last moment. Dostoevsky uses this story to show how the reprieved man's experience granted him a (metaphorical) resurrection, a new love for life. This experience has been internalized by Myshkin himself, who treats all whom he meets with a kind of love born of contact with death. On December 22, 1849, Dostoevsky and a number of acquaintances were taken from the Peter -and-Paul Fortress (where they had been imprisoned) to Semenovsky Square, read death sentences, prepared for execution by firing squad, and allowed to confess and kiss the cross. Dostoevsky and his cohorts had participated in a group that had discussed social change in Russia and read banned books. For the crime of their participation in the "Petrashevsky Circle" (named after their leader, at whose house they met), they were accused of plotting revolution and were sentenced to death. 23 As they prepared to die, Dostoevsky reportedly said (in French) to his friend

a number of acquaintances were taken from the Peter -and-Paul Fortress (where they had been imprisoned) to Semenovsky Square, read death sentences, prepared for execution by firing squad, and allowed to confess and kiss the cross. Dostoevsky and his cohorts had participated in a group that had discussed social change in Russia and read banned books. For the crime of their participation in the "Petrashevsky Circle" (named after their leader, at whose house they met), they were accused of plotting revolution and were sentenced to death. 23 As they prepared to die, Dostoevsky reportedly said (in French) to his friend among the group, Speshnev, "We will be with Christ (Nous serons avec le Christ)," to which Speshnev replied (in Russian), "Yes, a bit of dust." 24 Dostoevsky is also reported to have gone around noting parallels between what they were going through and Victor Hugo's Last Day of a Condemned Man, the first-person narrative that lasts right up to the moment when the protagonist is guillotined. After the first group of three - Petrashevsky, Grigoryev, and Mombelli, considered the three most guilty and therefore to be first executed - were blindfolded and ready to be shot, a messenger of Tsar Nicholas I rode up and read them a reprieve, which commuted their sentences to various terms of prison and exile. (Dostoevsky would spend five years, from 1850 to 1854, in at a prison labor camp in Tobolsk.) Later that day, back in prison at the Peter -and-Paul Fortress in Saint Petersburg, before beginning the trip to Siberia, Dostoevsky wrote to his brother of the experience, quoting loosely by memory from Hugo's work and heralding his own rebirth and new love for life. Having been that close to death, Dostoevsky felt that he had undergone a kind of rebirth. When Dostoevsky emerged years later from what he referred to as the "house of the dead" (prison camp), he again likened his experience to a "resurrection." Throughout the rest of his life, Dostoevsky drew in various ways on his experience on December 22, 1849. Having been "face -to-face" with death had, he felt, given him a new understanding of life. He imparts some of his understanding to his "holy fool" Myshkin. On the other hand, one of the Petrashevsky group, Nikolai Petrovich Grigoryev, who had been among the three already blindfolded when the reprieve was read, never recovered from this mock execution and the penal servitude that followed. He went mad. Being "face -to-face" with death and then "alive again" can make someone into a holy fool or it can bring on madness. Dostoevsky in The Idiot explores both of these possibilities in his portrayal of Myshkin. 7. Fatherhood and the Sting of the Grave Amid the pressures of writing The Idiot, in the face of their desperate finances, living in isolation, worried about Dostoevsky's health (physical and mental), the Dostoevskys looked forward quite joyously to the birth of their first child. When Dostoevsky went off gambling in October 1867, his letters to his wife contain affectionate references to "Misha" and/or "Sonya." In one letter, Dostoevsky opens by announcing that he has "lost everything" and continues to describe pawning his ring and coat, but then he cheerily asserts that he will find some way of covering "the expenses for our future guest and little angel." 25 --- Page 6 --- In her diary, Anna Grigoryevna reports Dostoevsky's solicitous anticipation. She tells of how the Swiss midwife asked them which of Dostoevsky's friends lived in her street; she had caught sight of Dostoevsky so often near her house that she assumed he must be visiting a neighbor of hers. It then came out that Dostoevsky had taken to walking to her house during his daily walks; he wanted to be thoroughly familiar with the route before the time came for him to summon her to assist at the birth. 26 Dostoevsky was proverbially bad at finding his way in strange cities. When his wife's labor began, Dostoevsky was asleep, recovering from an epileptic attack. His wife, reluctant to wake him, underwent the first hours of her labor while he slept. The next morning she finally awakened him and he went for the midwife. Dostoevsky gave her to understand that she was not showing the requisite concern and alacrity, to which she muttered, "O ces russes, ces russes!" But never, said this Swiss midwife to Anna Grigoryevna later, did a father show such feelings at the birth of his child, and she exclaimed, "O ces russes, ces russes!" some more. Dostoevsky's wife suggests that Dostoevsky modeled Shatov's reactions to his wife's labor, described in The Devils, on Dostoevsky's own. Sonya (Sophia) was born on February 2 2 /March 5, 1868. Writing especially to his friend Maikov (and Sonya's godfather to be) Dostoevsky revealed his feelings about fatherhood: "I have been feeling, for almost a month now, terribly much that is new and that until now has been unfamiliar to me, from exactly that moment that I first saw my Sonya, up to that moment when, through joint efforts, we just bathed her in a basin. Yes, an angelic soul has in fact flown in to be with us. But I won't describe my feelings to you. They are growing and developing with every day." 27 A bemused and very proud Dostoevsky describes how much his daughter resembles him: "She already even has exactly my

feelings about fatherhood: "I have been feeling, for almost a month now, terribly much that is new and that until now has been unfamiliar to me, from exactly that moment that I first saw my Sonya, up to that moment when, through joint efforts, we just bathed her in a basin. Yes, an angelic soul has in fact flown in to be with us. But I won't describe my feelings to you. They are growing and developing with every day." 27 A bemused and very proud Dostoevsky describes how much his daughter resembles him: "She already even has exactly my facial expression, my whole face, including the wrinkles on the brow - she lies there and looks as though she's writing a novel!" With his Saint Petersburg relatives, he was more guarded. But, in the beginning of May, Sonya took ill and died of pneumonia a week later. How did this death affect Dostoevsky? Dostoevsky's wife's memoirs tell of their shared sorrow and despair, and especially of her concern for her husband: "Deeply shaken and saddened by the loss, I was terribly afraid for my poor husband: his despair was stormy, he wept and cried like a woman; standing in front of the cold corpse of his loved one, he covered her pale face and hands with warm kisses. I never ever saw such stormy despair." 28 To Maikov, a grieving Dostoevsky wrote: "Where is Sonya? Where is that little human being for whom, I make bold to say, I would undergo suffering on the cross so that she might live?" 29 He continues a month later: "I will never forget and will never stop suffering! Even if we have another child, I don't understand how I will love him; where I will find love; I need Sonya. I cannot understand that she is no more and that I will never see her." 30 When his first wife died in 1864, Dostoevsky put the question to himself in his diary, "Masha is lying on the table. Will I see her again?" and tried to console himself by envisioning eternal life.31 And even just a few months before his daughter's death, Dostoevsky had comforted his relatives after the sudden death of A. P. Ivanov, the father of his niece Sonya, to whom The Idiot was dedicated. Dostoevsky assured the grieving family that they would "see" Ivanov again and admonished them not to give way to "atheism." But when his own Sonya died, Dostoevsky seems to have felt the sting of death and the victory of the grave more powerfully than ever before. How did this death affect The Idiot? In order to answer this question, it may be helpful to look ahead to The Brothers Karamazov, a novel that responds in overt ways to the death in 1878 of another of the Dostoevskys' children, their three -year-old Aleksei. to give way to "atheism." But when his own Sonya died, Dostoevsky seems to have felt the sting of death and the victory of the grave more powerfully than ever before. How did this death affect The Idiot? In order to answer this question, it may be helpful to look ahead to The Brothers Karamazov, a novel that responds in overt ways to the death in 1878 of another of the Dostoevskys' children, their three -year-old Aleksei. In this instance, Dostoevsky named the hero Aleksei after his dead son, and, as Robert Belknap has suggested, wrote the novel "to create a rich identity for the namesake of his dead son." 32 Furthermore, within the novel, consolation is offered for the death of children: Zosima counsels a grieving mother that with time her tears will turn to joy and asserts that Job can love his new children after his first ones have been murdered. The young Uyushechka's death gives meaning to the boys who will remember him and "see him again." And the novel's epigraph ("Verily, verily, I say unto you, except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit") suggests the fruitfulness of death. Robin Feuer Miller has written that "the theme of the lost child is as important to Dostoevsky's entire literary canon as it is to that of Dickens. The outpouring of grief for a dead, injured, or suffering child constitutes the groundswell of [The Brothers Karamazov]." 33 If Dostoevsky poured his own grief into the parts of The Idiot written after Sonya's death (which occurred when he was in the midst of part 2), he kept it beneath the surface of the novel, perhaps ultimately adding to the cathartic "groundswell" of grief that pours out in The Brothers Karamazov. Although it is impossible to say exactly how the death of Sonya figures in The Idiot, the fact that death appears increasingly as an inexorable and cruel force as the novel progresses may have been influenced by Dostoevsky's own despair. Where are the reprieves from death of the sort that Myshkin's Swiss tales, told to the Epanchins in part i, led the reader to expect? But perhaps the most disturbing change in the latter part of the novel is the emergence of Ippolit Terentiev, a youth dying of consumption, as a key figure. Joseph Frank calls him "the first in Dostoevsky's remarkable gallery of metaphysical rebels." Frank continues: "For Ippolit is revolting not against the iniquities of a social order but, anticipating Kirillov and Ivan Karamazov, against a world in which death, and hence immitigable human suffering, is an inescapable reality." 34 (Recall, in connection with the theme of dying children in particular, that Dostoevsky's most famous "metaphysical rebel" Ivan Karamazov adamantly refuses to accept the suffering and death of innocent children, and he rebels against Zosima's message of Job - like reconciliation.) In his confession, Ippolit calls into question Christ's resurrection. If, as

Frank calls him "the first in Dostoevsky's remarkable gallery of metaphysical rebels." Frank continues: "For Ippolit is revolting not against the iniquities of a social order but, anticipating Kirillov and Ivan Karamazov, against a world in which death, and hence immitigable human suffering, is an inescapable reality." 34 (Recall, in connection with the theme of dying children in particular, that Dostoevsky's most famous "metaphysical rebel" Ivan Karamazov adamantly refuses to accept the suffering and death of innocent children, and he rebels against Zosima's message of Job - like reconciliation.) In his confession, Ippolit calls into question Christ's resurrection. If, as Holbein's painting of the dead Christ suggests to Ippolit, men and women hope in vain for eternal life, then the whole universe becomes a dead machine that swallows life. 35 As Joseph Frank has reminded Dostoevsky's readers, Ippolit's interpretation of the Holbein painting is not the only one provided in the novel. 36 Earlier, in part 2 (in a section written before Sonya's death), Myshkin had responded to the same painting. While he admits that such a painting could cause one to lose faith, he goes on to tell Rogozhin several stories intended to document the faith and religious feeling of the Russian people. In one of these stories, a woman compares God's joy at the prayer of a sinner to a mother's joy at her baby's first smile (8:183 - 84). Thus, the novel provides an alternative to Ippolit's bleak view of a world devoid of faith in Myshkin's image of the uncanny faith of the Russian people. Indeed, Dostoevsky adheres throughout the novel to his "idea." What seems to have changed, in response to Sonya's death perhaps, is the sheer force with which death and human destruction are felt at the end of the novel and the introduction of the metaphysical rebel. For many readers, this metaphysical revolt has become the essence of Dostoevsky's works. Here again, however, Myshkin counters this revolt. When asked by a dying Ippolit how best to die, Myshkin gives what Joseph Frank calls "a moving and beautiful reply": 37 "Pass by us and forgive us our happiness!" (8:433). These words, said quietly by Myshkin within the novel, contain the essence of the message of reconciliation and consolation that Dostoevsky subsequently elaborates upon in The Brothers Karamazov. The "corns of wheat" dying in The Idiot would bear fruit in The Brothers Karamazov. 8. Italy and Dostoevsky's "Fantastic Prince" Soon after the death of Sonya, the Dostoevskys moved from Geneva to nearby Vevey and then to Italy in September 1868. Reluctant as they were to leave behind Sonya's grave, they were relieved to be gone from Switzerland. The peasants of Italy they met during their travels reminded them of Russian peasants; they provided a welcome antidote to the Swiss "bourgeois" about whom the Dostoevskys complained. They settled in Milan first and eventually in Florence, where they enjoyed the museums and sights. To Dostoevsky's relief, Anna Grigoryevna's spirits began to improve bit by bit. Work on

and Dostoevsky's "Fantastic Prince" Soon after the death of Sonya, the Dostoevskys moved from Geneva to nearby Vevey and then to Italy in September 1868. Reluctant as they were to leave behind Sonya's grave, they were relieved to be gone from Switzerland. The peasants of Italy they met during their travels reminded them of Russian peasants; they provided a welcome antidote to the Swiss "bourgeois" about whom the Dostoevskys complained. They settled in Milan first and eventually in Florence, where they enjoyed the museums and sights. To Dostoevsky's relief, Anna Grigoryevna's spirits began to improve bit by bit. Work on the novel progressed; Dostoevsky finished it by the end of 1868. As his work on the novel drew to a close, Dostoevsky grew more and more concerned about how the novel was being received back in Russia. He worried that the public did not like the novel and began to criticize the novel himself. His comments have sometimes been taken as proof that he regarded his novel as a failure. However, as with most of Dostoevsky's statements, it may be misleading to take them out of context. --- Page 7 --- All along, Dostoevsky depended largely on his friend Maikov for information. Writing to Dostoevsky shortly after the publication of the first section of the novel, Maikov begins: "I know, I know, I know, dear Fyodor Mikhailovich, I know that you are burning with impatience to know, how is the novel doing?" 38 Maikov reported that the novel was making a splash. In his next letter, written after he read the next installment (up to the end of Nastasya Filippovna's birthday party), Maikov voiced praise for the Idiot himself, deeming him to be "the most realistic character." "Does that seem strange to you?" Maikov adds. He then goes on say that all the other characters "live in a fantastic world, they all are illuminated by a strong, particular, but fantastic glow. You read it avidly but at the same time it does not seem believable." 39 Maikov here expresses hesitation about the "fantastic" aspect of his friend's novel. It was not the first time that Dostoevsky's works had been rejected as being "fantastic." Belinsky had objected to "the fantastic coloring" of Dostoevsky's The Double (1846), declaring "the fantastic in our day has its place only in madhouses, and not in literature, and it is in the purview of doctors, not of poets." 40 Dostoevsky, of course, refused to heed Belinsky's advice. In The Idiot Dostoevsky once again tears down the boundaries that Belinsky wanted to maintain between literature and the madhouse. No doubt, much about The Idiot would have struck Belinsky as being fantastic, and this may well have been Dostoevsky's intention as he wrote the novel: to embrace the very aspects of his own thematics and aesthetics that Belinsky objected to in The Double. Dostoevsky, as he settled down to write The Idiot after his article on Belinsky, may still have been settling scores with Belinsky. One of Dostoevsky's

In The Idiot Dostoevsky once again tears down the boundaries that Belinsky wanted to maintain between literature and the madhouse. No doubt, much about The Idiot would have struck Belinsky as being fantastic, and this may well have been Dostoevsky's intention as he wrote the novel: to embrace the very aspects of his own thematics and aesthetics that Belinsky objected to in The Double. Dostoevsky, as he settled down to write The Idiot after his article on Belinsky, may still have been settling scores with Belinsky. One of Dostoevsky's tactics when criticized was to take the criticism (you are calling my writing fantastic and you mean it as a slur) and make it into a cherished virtue (agreed, my writing is fantastic, and that is what makes it good). Whether in response to Maikov or the ghost of Belinsky, as he wrote The Idiot Dostoevsky increasingly identified his vision with the "fantastic." 41 When Dostoevsky's old friend, the critic Nikolai Strakhov, praised Myshkin, Dostoevsky wrote back begging for more opinions and then set about trying to explain what he wished to do in the novel. "I have my own special view of reality (in art), and what the majority calls almost fantastic and exceptional sometimes constitutes the very essence of the real. The ordinariness of phenomena and a banal view of them are not yet realism, in my opinion, but even the contrary." 42 Dostoevsky goes on to defend his "fantastic Idiot" who partakes of some transcendental reality. In the same paragraph, Dostoevsky says critical things about how the novel came out: "In the novel a lot was written in haste, much is dragged out and not successful, but there are some successful things too. I'm not defending my novel, but my idea." Readers and critics have tended to voice criticisms about the novel similar to those Dostoevsky expressed. What Dostoevsky says may be an accurate judgment of the novel or it may have been something that Dostoevsky did not fully believe. Dostoevsky was desperate for this novel to succeed, for financial reasons (so that a separate edition would be published, so that he would be paid more per page for the serial publication of his next novel) and also for the sake of his pride and all he had put into the novel, which was written under difficult conditions. As time went on, Dostoevsky was more willing to defend not just the "idea" of the novel but the novel itself. In 1877, for example, he responds to a reader who claimed to like The Idiot best of Dostoevsky's novels: "All those who speak to me of [The Idiot] in terms of its being the best of my works have something special in the cast of their minds that always touches me and pleases me." 43 Those who consider The Idiot to be their favorite of Dostoevsky's novels are likely to be those who welcome the "fantastic" - understood

In 1877, for example, he responds to a reader who claimed to like The Idiot best of Dostoevsky's novels: "All those who speak to me of [The Idiot] in terms of its being the best of my works have something special in the cast of their minds that always touches me and pleases me." 43 Those who consider The Idiot to be their favorite of Dostoevsky's novels are likely to be those who welcome the "fantastic" - understood in Dostoevsky's own sense of the term, as pertaining to a higher form of reality that embraces both life and death - in literature. 9. After The Idiot After Dostoevsky finished The Idiot, he and his wife moved on from Florence to Dresden, where they settled until their return to Russia. In Dresden, Anna Grigoryevna gave birth to their daughter Lyubov in September 1869. Dostoevsky finished The Eternal Husband and began work on The Devils. The Dostoevskys arrived back in Saint Petersburg during the summer of 1871, shortly before the birth of their son Fyodor. Upon return to Russia, Dostoevsky lived a much more settled life than before. The Dostoevsky family eventually divided its time between Saint Petersburg and the village of Staraya Russa. Dostoevsky made trips to Germany for his health. (During one of these trips, he visited Sonya's grave in Geneva.) Dostoevsky's exile from Russia renewed his emotional ties to his homeland and his dedication to the depiction of Russian life. Upon his return, he sought, more than ever before perhaps, to understand the full diversity of Russian life: women and men, young and old, common folk and intellectuals. Throughout the rest of his Russian life, he prided himself on not turning away from the chaos he saw around him. And, even while he was being lionized, he continued to take an active interest in his public. He would write to, or talk with, strangers who appealed to him for advice. He engaged in literary, social, religious, and political debates, as shown in his Diary of a Writer. He came to consider himself at this time the chronicler of the new Russian society. Surfacing in his work of this later period (The Adolescent [1875], The Brothers Karamazov [1879-80]), were his hopes for the resurgence of the Russian family and his faith in the "Godbearing" Russian people. The hopes developed during the period of exile and they were articulated in The Idiot, but along with uncertainty and doubt, as if the seeds of hope needed Russian soil to bear fruit. NOTES 1. Heroes, Heroines, and Their Relations The major characters of The Idiot are discussed below with respect to the meaning of their names and their family affiliation. In this novel, Dostoevsky appears to emphasize his characters' identities - who they are. (Plot - what the characters end up doing -becomes less crucial.) The names a person acquires at birth and the family she is born into seem to give that person a ready-made identity or, rather, a set of expectations about life. These are expectations, not fixed determinations. Dostoevsky was a master at playing on the expectations his characters' names and their family structures create. Dostoevsky's name -play often adds a symbolic level to the reader's understanding of a

meaning of their names and their family affiliation. In this novel, Dostoevsky appears to emphasize his characters' identities - who they are. (Plot - what the characters end up doing -becomes less crucial.) The names a person acquires at birth and the family she is born into seem to give that person a ready-made identity or, rather, a set of expectations about life. These are expectations, not fixed determinations. Dostoevsky was a master at playing on the expectations his characters' names and their family structures create. Dostoevsky's name -play often adds a symbolic level to the reader's understanding of a given character's identity. When Dostoevsky gives a character a meaningful name, he does so in order to evoke a set of questions about this character, not to define him or create an allegory or fixed correspondence. On a broader level, Dostoevsky rejected the notion of the "genetic" family, based entirely on blood and name, in favor of the "accidental" family, a structure where chance and love bind. 1 In this sphere as well, Dostoevsky creates a tension between what seems predetermined and what is not. The meanings and associations that inhere in the names of the major characters of the novel are discussed below. 2 The characters are grouped by family, but, given that Dostoevsky deals in "accidental" family units, the groupings become fluid. The case of Prince Lev Nikolaevich Myshkin is especially complex. As a "prince" he belongs to an old aristocratic line, but in his case one that now has little wealth or power. His last name is said in the novel to be found in Nikolai Karamzin's History of Russia, a fact that lends the aura of historical authority. And yet its Russian root is the same as that for "mouse." Hence it is associated with meekness and lack of power. His first name, Lev, means "lion." Lev combined with Myshkin makes an oxymoronic menagerie of lion and mouse -suggesting a fable with a moral, on the order of Aesop's "The Lion and the Mouse." His patronymic Nikolaevich (son of Nikolai, meaning "victory over the people" in Greek) further suggests that he was born with heroic expectations. But as an orphan, Lev Nikolaevich Myshkin was left on his own to interpret his legacy without the biological parents to whom he owes the names. The combination "Lev Nikolaevich" also belongs to Lev Nikolaevich Tolstoy, whose War and Peace had already started to be published in serial form. As Dostoevsky wrote his major novels, he was quite aware of Tolstoy's presence as a writer. It has been the fate of the two authors to be compared constantly to one another. Dostoevsky was forever gauging himself against Tolstoy and, of course, trying to outdo him. The fact that Nikolai Strakhov, Dostoevsky's old friend, later became increasingly devoted to Tolstoy served to divide rather than bring together the two writers. had already started to be published in serial form. As Dostoevsky wrote his major novels, he was quite aware of Tolstoy's presence as a writer. It has been the fate of the two authors to be compared constantly to one another. Dostoevsky was forever gauging himself against Tolstoy and, of course, trying to outdo him. The fact that Nikolai Strakhov, Dostoevsky's old friend, later became increasingly devoted to Tolstoy served to divide rather than bring together the two writers. Myshkin is an orphan and an "idiot" (the original Greek word [greek characters] ranging in meaning from private person - someone who stands apart from social structures - to an ill- informed man). Yet in the course of the novel, he discovers and develops various kinds of familial relationships with many of the families or individuals in the novel. As the orphaned outsider Myshkin affiliates himself with various families, we see Dostoevsky challenging the notion that family is exclusively a genetic unit. After the death of his parents, Myshkin had been under the guardianship of Nikolai Andreevich Pavlishchev (evoking Russian pavlin, "peacock"). Pavlishchev eventually entrusted Myshkin to the medical care of the Swiss doctor Schneider (from German schneiden, "to cut"). To what extent are these men responsible for Myshkin? Are they surrogate fathers? Do they fail Myshkin? What does father - hood mean? Dostoevsky seems to be searching for new definitions here. When a relative of his mother's dies, Myshkin inherits money. Again Dostoevsky asks what "family" means - Myshkin gets money from a distant relative he never knew simply because of a genetic relation. Further, Dostoevsky dramatizes the undeniable role that financial interdependence plays in binding families together. (Certainly money was central in Dostoevsky's relations with various family members.) The subplot involving "Pavlishchev's Son" further highlights the question of paternity (or relatedness in general) and what it means: a group of young nihilists try to con Myshkin out of part of his inheritance when one of them, Antip Burdovsky, claims (falsely, it turns out) to be the illegitimate son of Pavlishchev. ("Burdovsky," incidentally, has the same root as the Russian word for the slops that pigs eat. It suggests swinishness and, possibly, in an ironic twist, Dostoevsky's beloved parable of the Prodigal Son: the son, having demanded his inheritance of his father, squanders it and ends up sharing the swine's food, before returning, repentant, to his father.) The rationale for Burdovsky's claim is that Myshkin was getting money from Pavlishchev in the past that, by natural rights, should have gone to Pavlishchev's biological son. Now thatMyshkin has money, he is duty -bound to reimburse Pavlishchev's biological son. The irony here is that these nihilists are touting family values simply in an attempt to take advantage of Myshkin and to make money. Myshkin is distantly related to Lizaveta Prokofyevna Epanchin, who was a princess Myshkin (that is, from an old aristocratic family) before her marriage to Ivan Fyodorovich Epanchin, who advanced from humble origins to the rank of general. They have

Burdovsky's claim is that Myshkin was getting money from Pavlishchev in the past that, by natural rights, should have gone to Pavlishchev's biological son. Now thatMyshkin has money, he is duty -bound to reimburse Pavlishchev's biological son. The irony here is that these nihilists are touting family values simply in an attempt to take advantage of Myshkin and to make money. Myshkin is distantly related to Lizaveta Prokofyevna Epanchin, who was a princess Myshkin (that is, from an old aristocratic family) before her marriage to Ivan Fyodorovich Epanchin, who advanced from humble origins to the rank of general. They have three unmarried daughters: Alexandra, Adelaida, and Aglaya. The Epanchin parents have just recently abandoned their system of not pushing their daughters into marriage as they realize that Alexandra has "suddenly and almost unexpectedly" turned twenty -five. Aglaya, later in the novel, rebels against being married off. The Epanchin daughters' names all begin with the letter A and have Greek etymologies. The three sisters have been likened to the Three Graces. "Alexandra" comes from the Greek for "defend," "Adelaida" comes from the Greek for "obscure" (she is a painter who ends up marrying Prince Shch. - for whom a cipher-like initial takes the place of a name, a detail that makes him a good match for "obscure" Adelaida). "Aglaya" comes from the Greek, meaning "radiance." The symbolism of light becomes important later in the novel when Myshkin's love for her brings him the promise of a "new, radiant life." But, by the end of the novel, she has associated herself with the forces of darkness rather than life by cutting herself off from her family, marrying a Pole, and becoming a Catholic - in the Dostoevskian system these actions suggest that she has turned away from the light and is not living up to the promise of her name. A possible link has been suggested between her name and Blanche (from the French for "white"), for whose sake Marguerite forfeits her happiness in La Dame aux camélias (by Dumas the Younger). When Myshkin first arrives with his bundle on the Epanchins' doorstep, the General wants to send him away. In his initial conversation with Myshkin, he reveals his fears that Myshkin has come to sponge off of his relatives. Myshkin, however, disarms him. Eventually Epanchin relents and introduces him to the women of the family, his blood relations, who feel naturally drawn to him. Here Dostoevsky seems in fact to be suggesting that affinities are sometimes genetically determined. The Epanchin family is often cited as one of the few families in Dostoevsky's fiction where the nuclear family is intact (two parents and their shared offspring live under the same roof) and relatively happy. We are told that Epanchin never regretted his early marriage, that he "respected his wife and sometimes feared her to such an extent that he even loved her." If examined more carefully, this intact family may not be so perfect even at the beginning of the novel: Epanchin,

drawn to him. Here Dostoevsky seems in fact to be suggesting that affinities are sometimes genetically determined. The Epanchin family is often cited as one of the few families in Dostoevsky's fiction where the nuclear family is intact (two parents and their shared offspring live under the same roof) and relatively happy. We are told that Epanchin never regretted his early marriage, that he "respected his wife and sometimes feared her to such an extent that he even loved her." If examined more carefully, this intact family may not be so perfect even at the beginning of the novel: Epanchin, for example, buys Nastasya Filippovna expensive pearls for her birthday (which she returns, telling him: "Take these pearls and give them to your wife"), and he has developed a scheme for marrying Alexandra off to Totsky. Afanasy Ivanovich Totsky is a womanizer in his fifties. He has been, since the death of her parents, the guardian - and surrogate father - of Nastasya Filippovna Barashkov. (Her mother died in a fire; her shaken father died soon thereafter; her sister died --- Page 10 --- of whooping cough while under Totsky's care.) Totsky kept her living with various caretakers and eventually a Swiss governess in a country place called Otradnoe (a name shared with the Rostovs' beloved estate in War and Peace). The name of that estate, approximately "felicity" in Russian, evokes a pastoral idyll. But here when Nastasya was still quite young, Totsky seduced her. Thus the Totsky -Barashkov "accidental" grouping becomes a perversion of family and demonstrates the possibly disastrous outcome of the demise of a genetic family. When the action of the novel begins, Totsky is trying to marry Nastasya Filippovna to Gavrily (Ganya) Ivolgin, Epanchin's secretary, so that he, Totsky, can marry Alexandra Epanchin without being encumbered by Nastasya Filippovna. This plan to marry off Nastasya Filippovna is presented from Totsky's point of view as an attempt to regenerate and "resurrect" this fallen woman through "love and family" life (8:40-41). Here again, family values are touted, but with false motives. Nastasya Filippovna would rather free herself from all connec -tions to Totsky, who has been supporting her. Here again we see the interaction of family relations and financial dependence. On her birthday she declares: "I am the nameday girl and on my own for the first time in my whole life."3 She declares that she will let Myshkin decide her fate, refuses to marry Ganya Ivolgin, and then runs off with Rogozhin. Perhaps Dostoevsky intended Nastasya Filippovna's birthday celebration, the culmination of the first part of the novel, to contrast with the nameday celebration at the Rostovs' that occurs early in War and Peace, in a section Dostoevsky would have read before he wrote his opening to The Idiot. Tolstoy had used his party scene to show his Rostovs in all their glory; for Dostoevsky, these Rostovs are the "genetic" family par excellence. Even their name in Russian evokes the notion of biological growth. The celebration in

Myshkin decide her fate, refuses to marry Ganya Ivolgin, and then runs off with Rogozhin. Perhaps Dostoevsky intended Nastasya Filippovna's birthday celebration, the culmination of the first part of the novel, to contrast with the nameday celebration at the Rostovs' that occurs early in War and Peace, in a section Dostoevsky would have read before he wrote his opening to The Idiot. Tolstoy had used his party scene to show his Rostovs in all their glory; for Dostoevsky, these Rostovs are the "genetic" family par excellence. Even their name in Russian evokes the notion of biological growth. The celebration in War and Peace honors two generations of Rostov women named Natalya (meaning "birth"). It celebrates generational continuity and clan loyalty. On any number of levels, Dostoevsky opposes Nastasya Filip-povna's birthday celebration to the nameday celebration for the two Natalya Rostovs, using the contrast to suggest, among other things, the very different set of familial circumstances from which these two heroines, Natasha Rostov and Nastasya Filippovna, spring. All three of Nastasya Filippovna Barashkov's names have rich symbolism. Her last name, "Barashkov," comes from the Russian word for "lamb" and possibly evokes all of the Gospel sheep and Old Testament sacrificial lambs. Her patronymic, from the Greek for "lover of horses," evokes the horses of Revelation, which become a subject of discussion between her and Lebedev and others in the novel. Her first name, in its long form "Anastasiia," comes from the Greek word for "resurrection." (Its meaning is thus opposed to that of Natalya, meaning "birth.") The name "Anastasiia" is linked to the idea of the resurrection of the dead, which also becomes a topic of debate later in the novel. (Ippolit argues that looking at Holbein's painting of the dead Christ, it is impossible to believe that Christ was resurrected.) As a fallen woman, Nastasya is metaphorically "dead." When Myshkin comes offering the hope of rehabilitation and regeneration, Dostoevsky presents this in the text and in the notebooks as a "resurrection." Thus he suggests that the drama of Nastasya is whether or not the potential in her name will be realized. As it happens, she ends up murdered rather than resurrected or rehabilitated through Myshkin's love. The scene at which Rogozhin and Myshkin hold vigil over her murdered body evokes the death of Christ where the Marys held vigil. Jesus' shroud has been replaced by sturdy American oilcloth, and the ointment the Marys brought with them has been replaced by the "Zhdanov liquid" that Rogozhin uses, along with the oilcloth, to counter the smell of decay. "How can you call this [the Anastasiia under the oilcloth] Resurrection?" is the question this tableau poses. Parfyon Semyonovich Rogozhin, like Nastasya Filippovna, has a richly evocative name. His first name comes from the Greek word meaning "virgin." This name would seem not to fit, since Rogozhin represents a dark erotic force. Again, as with the first name "Anastasiia," the name goes counter to the reality but still evokes some sense of an ideal

ointment the Marys brought with them has been replaced by the "Zhdanov liquid" that Rogozhin uses, along with the oilcloth, to counter the smell of decay. "How can you call this [the Anastasiia under the oilcloth] Resurrection?" is the question this tableau poses. Parfyon Semyonovich Rogozhin, like Nastasya Filippovna, has a richly evocative name. His first name comes from the Greek word meaning "virgin." This name would seem not to fit, since Rogozhin represents a dark erotic force. Again, as with the first name "Anastasiia," the name goes counter to the reality but still evokes some sense of an ideal image of the person that has been violated. The last name "Rogozhin" comes from rogozha, the Russian word for "bast" or "bast mat," by metaphorical extension, something a bit shoddy. Curiously, the word is used in a proverb that invokes another family of The Idiot: "ne k rozhe rogozha, ne k litsu épancha" (a mat's not for the mug, a cloak's not for the face). 4 Dostoevsky may also have based the name "Rogozhin" on the name of a cemetery in Moscow associated with the Old Believers. Rogozhin's family is from the merchant class and linked to Russian sectarianism. 5 Rogozhin's father has died, leaving him money; again money and heritage are interconnected. Rogozhin's family lives in a creepy old house in Saint Petersburg that is windowless, with thick walls. There in her separate quarters Rogozhin's mother reads the Chet'i -Minei, a compendium of devotional literature, including saints' lives, arranged according to the calendar. In Rogozhin's quarters, Myshkin and Ippolit view the copy of Hans Holbein's Dead Christ. It is there that Myshkin notices a garden knife Rogozhin is using to cut the pages of a book. Rogozhin and Myshkin become "brothers" when they exchange crosses, an act that blurs the boundary between victimizer and victim. Rogozhin also at one point makes an attempt to murder Myshkin, stopped only when Myshkin has an epileptic attack. Yet Myshkin's devotion to his "brother" Rogozhin continues, surviving the attempted murder, the rivalry for Nastasya Filippovna, and even the murder of Nastasya Filippovna. At the price of his own sanity, perhaps, Myshkin stays with Rogozhin, stroking his cheek and caring for him after Nastasya Filippovna's death. Myshkin met Gavrily (Ganya) Ivolgin, the older son of the Ivolgin family, at the Epanchins and comes to them as their boarder, thus entering their family life. By showing the Ivolgins forced by finances to take in boarders, Dostoevsky again shows the boundaries of the family being redefined in response to external factors. Myshkin joins Ferdyshchenko (whose name hints at German Pferd, "horse") as a boarder. General Ivolgin, the head of this household, has gone to seed and remains obsessed with Napoleon, whom he claims to have served in his childhood during the siege of Moscow. A "Napoleon complex" may have been part of his patrimony to his son Ganya: when Ganya is first introduced at the Epanchins, he is said to have a "small, Napoleonic beard" (8:21).

showing the Ivolgins forced by finances to take in boarders, Dostoevsky again shows the boundaries of the family being redefined in response to external factors. Myshkin joins Ferdyshchenko (whose name hints at German Pferd, "horse") as a boarder. General Ivolgin, the head of this household, has gone to seed and remains obsessed with Napoleon, whom he claims to have served in his childhood during the siege of Moscow. A "Napoleon complex" may have been part of his patrimony to his son Ganya: when Ganya is first introduced at the Epanchins, he is said to have a "small, Napoleonic beard" (8:21). But the "Napoleonic beard" in question was a beard like that of Napoleon III, Napoleon's nephew who was the emperor of France at the time of the novel. Thus Ganya's Napoleonicism is a shoddier or diminished version of his father's. And Dostoevsky is suggesting that here, contrary to Darwin's theory, the sons (and nephews) are not an improvement over the fathers. Ganya, describing to Myshkin his scheme to get rich, shares his dream of how in the future people will say, "Behold, Ivolgin, King of the Jews," in imitation of the inscription on Jesus' cross. In addition, Dostoevsky draws here on Heinrich Heine's depiction of James Rothschild as the "King of the Jews." Alexander Herzen, in his Past and Thoughts, likewise had used the term "King of the Jews" for Rothschild in the context of a discussion of how money brings power and independence. 6 Dostoevsky thus presents Ganya as involved in an imitation of the wrong "King of the Jews." (Myshkin, on the other hand, imitates the correct King of the Jews.) Dostoevsky uses Ganya as an example of an "ordinary" person who wants desperately to be extraordinary. Ganya, along with his sister Varya (short for "Varvara," from Greek, "foreigner, barbarian") and the man she marries, Ivan Petrovich Ptitsyn (Russian ptitsa, "bird"), are the subject of the long discourse on "ordinary" people with which part 4 of the novel begins. The adolescent Kolya (short for "Nikolai") Ivolgin develops a close relationship with Myshkin. He nurses Myshkin after his epileptic attack, defends and protects Myshkin from the group of young nihilists, and seems to act under the influence of Myshkin when he helps the dying Ippolit. Myshkin is first introduced to Ippolit Terentiev, a boy dying of consumption, when General Ivolgin takes Myshkin to visit Ippolit's mother, Ivolgin's mistress. Ippolit later turns up as part of the band of nihilists who try to extract money from Myshkin. He reads his "Confession" at Prince Myshkin's birthday party at the Lebedev dacha. Ippolit eventually moves out to Pavlovsk, where he is cared for by Mrs. Ivolgin and Kolya. The name "Ippolit," meaning "looser of horses," may relate to the Horses of the Apocalypse that are discussed in the novel. 7 The fact that he and Nastasya Filippovna both have names containing the Greek root meaning "horse" links these two characters. Both show an active interest in the Apocalypse. Both are dead by
