Nas -tasya Filippovna's act of running away from Myshkin to Rogozhin, and her eventual death, may further condemn what Myshkin represents. --- Page 50 --- In the novel, characters such as Radomsky and Prince Shch. cast aspersions on Myshkin and his love. Radomsky reduces Myshkin's Christlike charity to "philanthropy" as he undermines the whole venture: "You see, Prince, being a philanthropist is pleasant, but not very" (8:261 -62). At another point, Prince Shch. informs Myshkin that enacting heaven on earth is harder than he thinks. Whatever specific form the criticisms leveled at Myshkin take, they essentially ask why things get worse rather than better in the presence of Myshkin's love. Similar concerns have arisen among readers of The Idiot, who perceive failure in Myshkin, or in the novel, or in Dostoevsky's attempt to imitate Christ. This critical response to his novel might not have surprised Dostoevsky, for he was familiar with this sort of attitude at least from the 1840s, the period of his friendship with Belinsky. (As Joseph Frank has suggested, Dostoevsky, who, prior to writing The Idiot, had been working on an article on Belinsky, may well have had Belinsky's views on Christ in mind as he set about to depict a Christlike man in The Idiot.) 17 In an entry in his Diary of a Writer in 1873, Dostoevsky noted that Belinsky, unlike other atheists such as Renan, rejected not only Christ's divinity but even the very notion that Christ was an ideal to be emulated. In Belinsky's view (or Dostoevsky's view of Belinsky's view), Jesus would not have had much of an effect had he lived in their present day (he probably would have joined forces with the socialist movement), and the imitation of Christ should not be set forth as man's goal because "it is absurd and even cruel to demand from man that which he, by the very laws of nature, could not live up to even if he wanted to" (21:11). As understood by Dostoevsky, the Belinskian viewpoint was that Christlike tactics fail to produce the desired effects (peace and justice for all), whereas a more savvy Realpolitik that expects less of men and women would be more successful. Radomsky's criticism of Myshkin concerning the lack of positive results produced by his Christlike love (with the implication that his love in fact does more harm than good) harks back to Belinsky's views on Jesus. At the same time, these moments in The Idiot where Myshkin's imitatio Christi is condemned also look forward to Ivan Karamazov's "Legend of the Grand Inquisitor." In both the denouement of The Idiot and that "legend," an advocate of good points out to (a) Christ

and justice for all), whereas a more savvy Realpolitik that expects less of men and women would be more successful. Radomsky's criticism of Myshkin concerning the lack of positive results produced by his Christlike love (with the implication that his love in fact does more harm than good) harks back to Belinsky's views on Jesus. At the same time, these moments in The Idiot where Myshkin's imitatio Christi is condemned also look forward to Ivan Karamazov's "Legend of the Grand Inquisitor." In both the denouement of The Idiot and that "legend," an advocate of good points out to (a) Christ (figure) his multiple failures to effect any good; in each case the Inquisitor suggests concrete steps that would, he argues, result in greater welfare. Jesus, the argument runs, seems to expect too much of men and women: they are not up to the challenge; hence chaos continues to reign. With a bit more authority, miracle, and bread, a better world would result. (Ultimately, these Inquisitors are of the "bread -to-stones" materialist mentality Dostoevsky was to decry in his last decade of writing.) In each case, (the) Christ (figure) turns the dialectic cheek and fails to argue back: Jesus kisses the Grand Inquisitor; Myshkin is silent and becomes a mute idiot. 18 Radomsky, accusing Myshkin of applying democratic, pro -woman-question tactics in his behavior toward Aglaya and Nastasya Filippovna, blames him for creating an impossible muddle and ruining their lives. When, a bit later, Nastasya Filippovna is murdered and Aglaya ends up married to a charlatan and converted to Catholicism, it would seem that Radomsky was right: Myshkin has only made everyone's lot worse. Readers of the novel and residents of Pavlovsk object, Why all the chaos and destruction in the wake of Myshkin? Many suggestions have surfaced to explain what goes wrong here. For example, in a recent dissertation,19 Marina Woronzoff-Dashkoff suggests that because Myshkin is a quixotic hero, his "moral understanding is thwarted by the failure to sympathize." While she may be right in her argument about Myshkin's failure to understand others, further "understanding" might not have altered the tragic denouement. (What possibilities for a happier ending are there, anyway? For a double wedding? For a socialist ménage à quatre?) Again, Dostoevsky in his writings appears to have anticipated some of the criticism leveled at the Idiot and The Idiot. For Dostoevsky, the fact that, alas, chaos, suffering, and destruction persist is not justification for denying Christ or Christianity. In his diary entry of Holy Thursday, 1864 ("Masha is lying on the table"), Dostoevsky contemplates the meaning for man of Christ's life and death, and declares Christlike love of another as oneself as the ideal toward which man strives. But then he adds: NB. The Antichrists are mistaken in trying to refute Christianity by means of the following major objections: 1) "Why is it that if Christianity is true it does not reign on earth; why do men continue to suffer, why have they not become brothers to each

destruction persist is not justification for denying Christ or Christianity. In his diary entry of Holy Thursday, 1864 ("Masha is lying on the table"), Dostoevsky contemplates the meaning for man of Christ's life and death, and declares Christlike love of another as oneself as the ideal toward which man strives. But then he adds: NB. The Antichrists are mistaken in trying to refute Christianity by means of the following major objections: 1) "Why is it that if Christianity is true it does not reign on earth; why do men continue to suffer, why have they not become brothers to each other?" In fact it is quite obvious why: because it is an ideal for the future, ultimate life of man, whereas on earth man is in a transitional state. It will come into being but only after the goal has been achieved, when man has by the laws of nature been definitively reborn as another nature that does not marry and is not given in marriage and, second, Christ Himself preached his teaching only as an ideal, He Himself foretold that until the end of the world there would be struggle and development (the teaching about the sword), for this is a law of nature, because on earth life is in the process of developing, whereas there [what awaits is] an existence that is one of total synthesis, eternally delightful and full, for which, therefore, "there will be time no more." The above passage is relevant to The Idiot in more ways than one. Dostoevsky makes it clear that his Jesus comes wielding a sword, 20 and that the strife will continue, literally, until kingdom come. Even if Easter and resurrection promise that in the long run the strife may be over and the battle won, in the short run, until the Second Coming, the struggle continues, despite mankind's efforts to love. Further, this ongoing strife, in Dostoevsky's view, does not nullify Christ or his teaching. Central both to Dostoevsky's conception of Myshkin as a Christlike figure and to the objections to Myshkin in the novel is the way Myshkin imitated Jesus' treatment of the sinning woman brought to him by the scribes and Pharisees for judgment (John 8:3 -11).21 They lead her in, announcing: "Now in the law Moses commanded us to stone such. What do you say about her?" Several entries in Dostoevsky's notebooks for the novel suggest that this passage was very much on his mind as he wrote The Idiot and that Myshkin's treatment of Nastasya Filippovna was, indeed, in imitation of Jesus' behavior here. In the novel itself, Myshkin, referring to Nastasya Filippovna, tells Aglaya not to throw a stone at her (8:361). But Radomsky, the raisonneur of the novel, 22 chides Myshkin for having carried forgiveness of Nastasya Filippovna too far and turned it into approval of her behavior. Radomsky believes that a tougher love would yield better results. Radomsky argues that Myshkin's imitation of how Jesus acted in John 8 is flawed. He

that this passage was very much on his mind as he wrote The Idiot and that Myshkin's treatment of Nastasya Filippovna was, indeed, in imitation of Jesus' behavior here. In the novel itself, Myshkin, referring to Nastasya Filippovna, tells Aglaya not to throw a stone at her (8:361). But Radomsky, the raisonneur of the novel, 22 chides Myshkin for having carried forgiveness of Nastasya Filippovna too far and turned it into approval of her behavior. Radomsky believes that a tougher love would yield better results. Radomsky argues that Myshkin's imitation of how Jesus acted in John 8 is flawed. He tells Myshkin: "But, you know, of course, she [the woman taken in adultery] was not told that she was doing the right thing, that it was worthy of all sorts of honors and respect" (8:482). Radomsky essentially feels that Myshkin has taken a dangerous step in turning sympathy and forgiveness into approval and praise, whereas Christ, Radomsky implies, stopped short of this. Is Radomsky right or is Radomsky a bit of a scribe and a Pharisee in wanting the sinning woman not to get off too easily? It should be noted that, while John has Jesus conclude by saying "Go and sin no more" to the adulterous woman, Jesus explicitly does not judge her. Having challenged whoever of the scribes and Pharisees was "without sin" to cast the first stone, Jesus waits while they file out one by one, "convicted by their own conscience." Then he asks the woman: "Where are those thine accusers? Hath no man condemned thee?" When she answers, "No man, Lord," he tells her, "Neither do I condemn thee: go and sin no more." In the tradition of Russian Orthodoxy, a noncondemning love of the sinner was the ideal. "He who has achieved true prayer and love is incapable of making distinctions; he does not distinguish the righ - teous from the sinner, but loves all equally and does not judge, like God himself; the sun shines and rain falls on the just and on the unjust," declares the narrator of Tales of a Pilgrim Told to His Spiritual Father, weaving in paraphrases from the Philokalia.23 This type of love - love that "does not make a distinction of things" - runs counter to earthly, worldly notions of justice, bred in the bone. Myshkin exemplifies this type of love in that he refuses to distinguish between sinner and righteous woman. In so doing, however, he violates people's sense of order and form and promotes earthly chaos. In Adultery in the Novel, Tony Tanner discusses the tension in novels of adultery between the Christlike forgiveness of the sinning woman, as described in John 8, and the scribe -like and Pharisee-like desire to stone her. 24 Tanner argues that the bourgeois novel of adultery depends largely on the tension that develops as the novel seeks to incorporate both responses to adultery: "a strictness that works to maintain the law, and a sympathy and understanding with the adulterous violator

sinner and righteous woman. In so doing, however, he violates people's sense of order and form and promotes earthly chaos. In Adultery in the Novel, Tony Tanner discusses the tension in novels of adultery between the Christlike forgiveness of the sinning woman, as described in John 8, and the scribe -like and Pharisee-like desire to stone her. 24 Tanner argues that the bourgeois novel of adultery depends largely on the tension that develops as the novel seeks to incorporate both responses to adultery: "a strictness that works to maintain the law, and a sympathy and understanding with the adulterous violator that works to undermine it" (14). And indeed, "it is arguable that it is just such a tension between law and sympathy that holds the great bourgeois novel together, and a severe imbalance in either direction must destroy the form" (14). If Tanner's assumptions about "the great bourgeois novel" are applied to The Idiot, then it just may be that the formal problems many critics have seen in The Idiot result from "severe imbalance" between sympathy and law. 25 But perhaps it is an error to judge The Idiot by the standards of the bourgeois novel of adultery. The Idiot presents a main hero and, I would argue, an author, who both tip the scale toward compassion and a refusal to condemn, no matter what the destruction all this brings to traditional forms. This suggests that this novel is, indeed, different from the "great bourgeois" novels Tanner discusses. (According to Tanner, lack of the prescribed tension between sympathy and law results in a degeneration into sexuality or "linguicity," 26 -but this does not quite fit --- Page 51 --- Dostoevsky, although there may be elements of each.) Rather, in The Idiot, Dostoevsky sets forth a new type of novel, the novel of the accidental family, a family he himself in Diary of a Writer opposed to the genetic family, whose chronicler was Tolstoy. The novel of the accidental family, as Dostoevsky understood it, had its own poetics, which Dostoevsky commented on, directly and indirectly, in his works. 27 In Dostoevsky's view, new artistic forms had to be created to represent disorder and decay. In order to arrive at these, however, the writer had to "guess and . . . make mistakes" (13:455). 28 In The Idiot, a novel with a Christlike hero, Dostoevsky works out these new forms, new forms that happen to bear some resemblance to the chronicle of Christ's life: the New Testament. Much of the end of The Idiot is devoted to the narrator's throwing up his hands at the task of chronicling the last days of the action. Rather than go in search of the historical events or attempt to create a coherent narrative, the narrator chooses to give us several different reports, based on different accounts, and with various inconsistencies and contradictions. 29 Is this not a bit like the narratives of Jesus's life? Many readers, including Tolstoy and Strakhov, have been frustrated and have

that happen to bear some resemblance to the chronicle of Christ's life: the New Testament. Much of the end of The Idiot is devoted to the narrator's throwing up his hands at the task of chronicling the last days of the action. Rather than go in search of the historical events or attempt to create a coherent narrative, the narrator chooses to give us several different reports, based on different accounts, and with various inconsistencies and contradictions. 29 Is this not a bit like the narratives of Jesus's life? Many readers, including Tolstoy and Strakhov, have been frustrated and have wanted one harmonious version of what happened in and about Pavlovsk during the White Nights of 1868 and of what happened in and around Jerusalem in 33. But, for Dostoevsky, if you choose Christ or an imitation of Christ, you must be willing to forgo having one "true" version. And you will spend your time "looking through a murky glass, guessingly," until there is time no more, when you will "see face to face." The accidental family, whose chronicle, by necessity, needed new forms and new poetics, differs from the "genetic" family, where genealogy and patrimony drive the narrative. The accidental family is a family born out of the decomposition of the genetic family and one that is bound together by love rather than blood and genes. Perhaps Dostoevsky even regarded Jesus as the founder of the accidental family: according to John's Gospel, Jesus' last act before dying on the cross is to bind his grieving loved ones, who have to watch him die, all the while knowing, like Pascal's convicts, that their turn will come. Jesus tells his mother, "Woman, behold your son!" refer - ring to John ( "the disciple standing by whom he loved") and he tells John, "Behold, your mother!" - from which point on John "took her into his own home" (John 19:26 -27). Here perhaps is Jesus' most pro -family act of all: here he counterbalances the damage that he had done to families throughout his ministry when he announced that he had come to wield his sword and set families against each other (Matt. 10:34-35). The "family" Jesus creates provides a model for the accidental family whose poet -chronicler Dostoevsky saw himself becoming. In his Diary of a Writer Dostoevsky notes that the "old form" of the Russian family is gone, and he asks: "Does the family have the strength to form a new and desirable form that satisfies the Russian heart?" (25:173). For Dostoevsky, such a family would be a family where the balance is tipped toward compassion and forgiveness, and the disorder that results. The "Russian heart," in Dostoevsky's view, had a strong capacity for these things. Dostoevsky, in the end of The Idiot, depicts the Epanchin family, as well as the Lebedev family, as just such families. Although shaken by all the "terror" it has lived through, the Epanchin family has survived in a new form and seems to count

asks: "Does the family have the strength to form a new and desirable form that satisfies the Russian heart?" (25:173). For Dostoevsky, such a family would be a family where the balance is tipped toward compassion and forgiveness, and the disorder that results. The "Russian heart," in Dostoevsky's view, had a strong capacity for these things. Dostoevsky, in the end of The Idiot, depicts the Epanchin family, as well as the Lebedev family, as just such families. Although shaken by all the "terror" it has lived through, the Epanchin family has survived in a new form and seems to count as one of its members their distant relative and the former fiancé of Aglaya, the "idiot" Myshkin, of whom we are told: "Apparently, he was forgiven for everything." By prefacing the news of this forgiveness with "apparently," the narrator makes some judgment about what he conveys, and he betrays his own possibly less forgiving heart. (This "apparently" has much the same effect as Ra -domsky's complaints that Myshkin overdid his imitation of Jesus' forgiving the sinning woman.) In Dostoevsky and "The Idiot," Robin Feuer Miller discusses the effects resulting from the division Dostoevsky creates between the author (who has a more forgiving heart) and the narrator (who is more prone to judgment). She argues that the reader is, to some degree, left to choose sides. In this respect The Idiot is much like Madame Bovary, another novel where John 8 is a subtext and readers find themselves, willy -nilly, in the position of scribes and Pharisees, who must decide whether to condemn or not condemn Emma. Flaubert achieves this effect of drawing the reader into this role by opening his novel in the French schoolroom where the new pupil Charles Bovary (who arrives on the scene as a definite outsider, wearing a funny hat, rather like the way Myshkin will arrive in Saint Petersburg) is met with the derision and cruelty of his scribe -like classmates who pelt Charles with spitballs. Throughout the novel, Flaubert forces his readers to decide whether to throw stones at Emma, to throw spitballs at Charles Bovary, to join in the cries of "charivari" that both his name and the action of the novel seem to invite - or to look into their own conscience and refrain from punishing others. 30 Dostoevsky challenges his reader no less than does Flaubert. Dostoevsky seems to have understood the second layer to the story of John 8: whereas it seems initially that the drama (or miracle) is what transpires between Jesus and the sinning woman, the essence of what Jesus has done in this scene is ultimately the change undergone in the consciences of the scribes and Pharisees, who "convicted by their own conscience, went out one by one," without casting a stone. 31 Certainly Dostoevsky makes it tempting for readers to join those residents of Pavlovsk, including the novel's narrator, who are ready to join in the charivari if Myshkin marries (8:491) or to cast spitballs and

the second layer to the story of John 8: whereas it seems initially that the drama (or miracle) is what transpires between Jesus and the sinning woman, the essence of what Jesus has done in this scene is ultimately the change undergone in the consciences of the scribes and Pharisees, who "convicted by their own conscience, went out one by one," without casting a stone. 31 Certainly Dostoevsky makes it tempting for readers to join those residents of Pavlovsk, including the novel's narrator, who are ready to join in the charivari if Myshkin marries (8:491) or to cast spitballs and stones when he does not. But Dostoevsky, imitating Jesus in John 8, challenges us to convict ourselves in our own consciences. At this point, we land ourselves in the Pascalian cachot Dostoevsky's novel so convincingly re -creates, and we risk becoming idiots. In this cachot, Dostoevsky wants us to realize that love is the only possible response to death and that this love can make us behold our mother, or our son. Afterword: From The Idiot to The Brothers Karamazov Dostoevsky remained loyal to his beloved "fantastic idiot" in the face of the criticism heaped on his novel and his own doubts about its success, but he would also go on in The Adolescent and The Brothers Karamazov to explore other ways to be "positively beautiful" and Christlike: ones perhaps less true to the spirit of the whole New Testament (which Myshkin embodies) and more in the spirit of the religious tradition developed by the Russian people, whom Dostoevsky (and Myshkin) prized so highly. In The Adolescent, in the pilgrim Makar Dolgoruky, Dostoevsky presents a positively beautiful man, whose meek, all -forgiving, nonconvicting love miraculously comforts and inspires his (accidental) family, when he comes "home" to them to die. And in The Brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky presents other versions of a positively beautiful man in the elder Zosima and in the novice Alyosha Karamazov, who leaves the monastery at his elder's behest in order better to love his brothers. In "The Aesthetics of Russian Folk Religion and The Brothers Karamazov," Shanti Elliott identifies and discusses the difference between the elder (the bearer of Dostoevsky's religious vision in The Brothers Karamazov) and the holy fool (whom Myshkin resembles): the holy fool, as an idiot, stands apart, outside of the community, and challenges it in a static way, but the elder, still using elements of holy foolery, builds community and responds to suffering in a more overtly constructive way. 32 Like The Idiot, The Brothers Karamazov was written as Dostoevsky grieved over the death of a child. 33 When Dostoevsky's three -year-old son, Alyosha, died (of what was believed to be epilepsy), Dostoevsky went with Vladimir Solovyov on a pilgrimage to the monastery of Optina Pustyn. There he was comforted in his grief by the elder Amvrosy, in much the same way as Zosima comforts the peasant woman who comes to him grieving over the loss of her three -year-old son. Whereas the

elements of holy foolery, builds community and responds to suffering in a more overtly constructive way. 32 Like The Idiot, The Brothers Karamazov was written as Dostoevsky grieved over the death of a child. 33 When Dostoevsky's three -year-old son, Alyosha, died (of what was believed to be epilepsy), Dostoevsky went with Vladimir Solovyov on a pilgrimage to the monastery of Optina Pustyn. There he was comforted in his grief by the elder Amvrosy, in much the same way as Zosima comforts the peasant woman who comes to him grieving over the loss of her three -year-old son. Whereas the sting of death is felt brutally throughout The Idiot, in The Brothers Karamazov there are moments when the novel seems to echo Paul in asking, "O Death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?" (1 Cor. 15:55). The Idiot boldly asks whether facing death (one's own or that of a loved one) does not make one lose one's faith in God (without which any imitation of Christ is meaningless, for Dostoevsky). In this regard, Dostoevsky echoes Hugo's Notre Dame de Paris, where Es- méralda's mother, who believes Esmeralda has been murdered, declares that "a mother who has lost a child no longer believes in God" in her prayer to Mary, whom she addresses because Mary, too, has been a grieving mother. But in Hugo's Notre Dame de Paris, this mother miraculously gets her child back; further, Hugo presents the cathedral, where the action of the novel takes place, as a symbol of faith in 1482, a faith maintained even in the face of dying children. Admittedly, Hugo's concern in Notre Dame de Paris is that the printing press, the "Mother - Revolution" will (in his words) "kill" the cathedral and all it represents. The printing press, by allowing any individual to reproduce and disseminate his word, will "kill" the collective faith --- Page 52 --- and spirit that built cathedrals such as Notre Dame. In The Idiot, Dostoevsky picks up where Hugo left off, asking whether faith is possible in the postrevolutionary world of the printing press. In modern Europe and even in the Russia he depicts, Hans Holbein's 1521 painting Christ in the Tomb and - even worse, but exactly proving Hugo's point - copies of this painting seem to have replaced the Cathedral of Notre Dame as an expression of man's artistic response to death. For Ippolit, this painting makes the universe into a machine that swallows Christ; Myshkin, however, finds in the Russian people a capacity for faith that transcends even Holbein's Christ. 34 In The Brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky presents the icon of the Mother of God as an emblem of the Russian people's faith (the faith that Myshkin attempted to describe in words to Rogozhin). The Mother of God comforts those grieving over dead children and those fearing death, in general. The devotion of the Russian people to the Mother of God is proverbial and, as Shanti Elliott discusses in her work, the

For Ippolit, this painting makes the universe into a machine that swallows Christ; Myshkin, however, finds in the Russian people a capacity for faith that transcends even Holbein's Christ. 34 In The Brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky presents the icon of the Mother of God as an emblem of the Russian people's faith (the faith that Myshkin attempted to describe in words to Rogozhin). The Mother of God comforts those grieving over dead children and those fearing death, in general. The devotion of the Russian people to the Mother of God is proverbial and, as Shanti Elliott discusses in her work, the use Dostoevsky makes of this aspect of Russian spirituality determines the aesthetics and ethics of The Brothers Karamazov. Here again we see Dostoevsky availing himself of other, perhaps more comforting, ways, of imitating Christ than those set forth in The Idiot. Recent studies have argued, following Dostoevsky's wife's observations, that the Gospel of John was Dostoevsky's favorite and the most influential.35 While it may have been his favorite all along, perhaps only in The Brothers Karamazov (and Crime and Punishment, earlier) did he allow its good news to be heard loud and clear. In this regard, his choice of John 12:24 as an epigraph to The Brothers Karamazov is significant; it proclaims the fruitfulness of death: "Verily, verily, I say unto you, except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit." The Brothers Karamazov ends with a funeral feast that recalls the celebration of the Eucharist at the Last Supper (as recorded by John), at which Alyosha attempts to convince Kolya Krasotkin (in the role of John's materialist doubting Thomas) that they will indeed see their dead friend Ilyushechka again in heaven. In contrast, Dostoevsky ends The Idiot with a murkier message, not wholly comforting, one that preserves all the complexity and disturbing ambiguity of the New Testament: one that combines John 19:26-27, where Mary is told "Behold thy son" and John is told "Behold thy mother," with Mark 16:8, where the Marys find the empty tomb and flee, "for trembling and astonishment had come upon them; and they said nothing to any one, for they were afraid." When Charles Bovary is asked his name by the teacher, he has trouble articulating it, is asked to repeat it, and finally "hurls out one word: Charbovari." "Charbovari" evokes, as commentary on the novel has noted, the word "charivari": the term for the mocking ritual celebration of "serenading" newlyweds on their wedding night by making a loud racket (banging on pots and pans and so forth) under their windows. Couples considered to be incongruous were especially likely targets of charivari. Flaubert thus imparts a kind of onomastic destiny to Charles's name - or at least to the way he pronounces it. The fact that Charles's schoolmates then begin to taunt him by crying "Charbovari! Charbovari!" enacts a kind of charivari. The scene suggests that it is Charles's destiny to be cuckolded and mocked. 31. Tony Tanner (Adultery, 21-22) writes of this passage, which is one of the cornerstones of his interpretation of the "novel of adultery": "Christ refuses to look and, initially, refuses to talk. That is, he refuses to participate in his purely specular attitude to the woman and to discuss her as a category. By doing this he restores the full existential reality to the situation that society seeks to deny. By treating the woman as spectacle and category, die representatives of society attempt to alienate her from her own being and to separate themselves from her by adopting the role of being the community from which the woman by her offense has isolated herself. Christ refuses to participate in their discourse, and when he does speak it is to them directly. This has two effects. Notebook: April 16/28, 1864 The passage below was written by Dostoevsky on Holy Thursday, 1864, as his first wife awaited burial. The ideas about love, death, and eternal life are given artistic expression and novelistic form in The Idiot. All four of the articles in the Criticism section of this volume refer to this seminal passage from Dostoevsky's notebook. F M. Dostoevskii, Polnoe sobranie socbinenii v tridtsati tomakh, ed. V. G. Bazanov et al. (Leningrad: Nauka, 1973), 20:172-75. April 16. Masha is lying on the table. Will I see Masha again? To love a person, as oneself, according to Christ's commandment, is impossible. The law of the self is binding on earth. The I stands in the way. Only Christ was able to, but Christ was the eternal ideal of ages, toward which man strives and must strive, according to the law of nature. Further, after the appearance of Christ, the ideal of man in flesh, it became as clear as day that the highest, the final development of the self must exactly (at the end of development, at the very moment of achieving the goal) get to the point when man will find, recognize, and with all the strength of his nature become convinced that the highest use to which man could put his self and the full development of his I - that is to destroy that I, to give it away in its entirety to each and everyone, completely and unconditionally. And that is the greatest happiness. In this way, the law of I merges with the law of humanism, and in that merger, both the I and the all (evidently

the end of development, at the very moment of achieving the goal) get to the point when man will find, recognize, and with all the strength of his nature become convinced that the highest use to which man could put his self and the full development of his I - that is to destroy that I, to give it away in its entirety to each and everyone, completely and unconditionally. And that is the greatest happiness. In this way, the law of I merges with the law of humanism, and in that merger, both the I and the all (evidently two extreme opposites), are mutually destroyed for each other, while at the same time they achieve, each in its own way, the highest goal of individual development. And that is what the paradise of Christ is. All of history, whether of mankind or even to some extent of each person separately, is just a process, a struggle, a striving for and approach to this goal. But if this [is the] ultimate goal of mankind (once it is attained, mankind will not have to develop, that is, to strive, to struggle, in the face of all his failures to glimpse the ideal and eternally to strive for it; that is, there'll be no need to live), then, consequently, man, by striving, defines his earthly existence. Thus, man on earth is a being necessarily developing, accordingly, not finished, but transitional. But to strive for such a great goal, to my way of thinking, would be utterly senseless if, upon reaching the goal, all is extinguished and disappears; that is, if man does not also have life after he reaches the goal. Consequently, there is a future, heavenly life. What it is like, where it is, on what planet, in what center, in the ultimate center, that is in bosom of the universal synthesis, that is, of God? - we don't know. We know only one feature of the future nature of the future being, which will hardly be called man (accordingly, we don't have a clue what kind of beings we will be). This feature has been foretold and guessed by Christ - the great and ultimate ideal of the development of all mankind - who appeared to us, in accordance with the law of our history, in flesh; that feature: "They neither marry nor are given in marriage but live as angels of God" - this is a deeply meaningful feature. 1) They do not many and are not given in marriage - because there's no reason; to develop, to strive for the goal whereby one generation succeeds another is no longer necessary and 2) Marriage and giving a woman in marriage is the most extreme rejection of humanism, the complete isolation of the couple from everyone (little is left over for everyone else). The family - that's the law of nature, but it is nevertheless an abnormal, egoistic (in every sense of the word) condition of man. The family -

angels of God" - this is a deeply meaningful feature. 1) They do not many and are not given in marriage - because there's no reason; to develop, to strive for the goal whereby one generation succeeds another is no longer necessary and 2) Marriage and giving a woman in marriage is the most extreme rejection of humanism, the complete isolation of the couple from everyone (little is left over for everyone else). The family - that's the law of nature, but it is nevertheless an abnormal, egoistic (in every sense of the word) condition of man. The family - that is the greatest holy of man on earth, for by means of this law of nature, man strives for the development of his goal (that is, by means of the succession of generations). But at the same time man must, by the same law of nature, in the name of the ultimate ideal of his goal, constantly deny it. (Duality). NB. The Antichrists are mistaken in trying to refute Christianity by means of the following major objections: 1) "Why is it that if Christianity is true it does not reign on earth; why do men continue to suffer, why have they not become brothers to each other?" In fact it is quite obvious why: because it is an ideal for the future, ultimate life of man, whereas on earth man is in a transitional state. It will come into being but only after the goal has been achieved, when man has by the laws of nature been definitively reborn as another nature that does not marry and is not given in marriage and, second, Christ Himself preached His teaching only as an ideal, He Himself foretold that until the end of the world there would be struggle and development (the teaching about the sword), for this is a law of nature, because on earth life is in the process of developing, whereas there [what awaits is] an existence that is one of total synthesis, eternally delightful and full, for which, therefore, "there will be time no more." NB2. The atheists, in rejecting God and the future life, are horribly inclined to present it all in human terms, and that is how they err. The nature of God is diametrically opposed to the nature of man. As a result of science man is going from multiplicity to the Synthesis, from facts to generalization and comprehension. But the nature of God is different. It is the complete synthesis of all being, self-contemplating itself in multiplicity, in Analysis. But if man is not man - what will his nature be? It is impossible to understand that on earth, but its law can be sensed both by all of mankind through its indirect manifestations (Prudhomme, the origin of God), and by each individual person. It is the merger of the full I, that is, of knowledge and synthesis with all. "Love all like oneself." That is impossible on earth, for it contradicts the

facts to generalization and comprehension. But the nature of God is different. It is the complete synthesis of all being, self-contemplating itself in multiplicity, in Analysis. But if man is not man - what will his nature be? It is impossible to understand that on earth, but its law can be sensed both by all of mankind through its indirect manifestations (Prudhomme, the origin of God), and by each individual person. It is the merger of the full I, that is, of knowledge and synthesis with all. "Love all like oneself." That is impossible on earth, for it contradicts the law of the development of the self and the striving for the final goal, by which man is bound. Accordingly, it is not an ideal law, as the Antichrists say, but the law of our ideal. NB. Thus, it all hinges on this: Christ is taken as the ultimate ideal on earth; that is, it all depends on Christian faith. Should you believe in Christ, then you believe that you will live forever. Is there future life for any I in that case? They say that man disintegrates and dies entirely. We already know that it can't be entirely, because man, just as when he gives birth physically to a son, he transfers to him some part of his self, so also morally he leaves a memory of himself to people (NB. The wish for eternal memory at memorials is significant); that is, man, through a part of his former self that lived on earth, enters into the future development of mankind. We can see clearly that the memory of great teachers of man lives among people (as does the development of villains), and even for man it is the greatest happiness to follow after them. That means that part of their natures goes by flesh and spirit into other people. Christ has entirely gone into mankind, and man strives to transform into the I of Christ as his ideal. If he should achieve that, he would clearly see that everyone who has this goal on earth has come to participate in the makeup of his final nature, that is, in Christ. (The synthetic nature of Christ is astounding. After all that is the nature of God, so, Christ is the reflection of God on earth.) Just how each I will be resurrected then - in the universal Synthesis - is difficult to imagine. But what is living, what has not died even before the final attainment and what is reflected in the final ideal - that must revive in the life that is final, synthetic, eternal. We will be people, constantly merging with everything, not giving in marriage and not marrying, in various arrangements (in my father's house are many mansions). All will then perceive and know itself for all time. But how that will be, in what form, in what nature - it is difficult for man to formulate for himself definitively. --- Page 55 --- And so,

is difficult to imagine. But what is living, what has not died even before the final attainment and what is reflected in the final ideal - that must revive in the life that is final, synthetic, eternal. We will be people, constantly merging with everything, not giving in marriage and not marrying, in various arrangements (in my father's house are many mansions). All will then perceive and know itself for all time. But how that will be, in what form, in what nature - it is difficult for man to formulate for himself definitively. --- Page 55 --- And so, man strives on earth toward an ideal counter to his nature. When man has not fulfilled the law of striving toward this ideal, that is, has not through love sacrificed his I to other people (I and Masha), then he experiences suffering and has called this state sin. And so, man must constantly experience suffering, which is counterbalanced by the heavenly delight of fulfilling the law, that is, through sacrifice. Herein lies the earthly balance. Otherwise the earth would be without meaning. The teaching of the materialists - universal inertia and the mechanics of matter - is death. The teaching of the true philosophy is the annihilation of inertia, it is the thought, it is the center and Synthesis of the universe and its external form, matter, it is God, it is eternal life. 2. Anna Dostoevsky, "Geneva (1868)" In the passage below Dostoevsky's wife describes Dostoevsky as father to their first child and his response to her death. A. G. Dostoevskaia, Vospominaniia A. G. Dostoevskoi, ed. L. P. Grossman (Moscow and Leningrad: Gosdarstvennoe izd., 1925. Reprint: [n.p.]: Gregory Lounz Books, 1969), 121-22. When a certain order had been established, there began a life that left fond memories forever. To my great happiness, Fyodor Mikhailovich turned out to be a very tender father: he always was there during her bath helping me, he himself wrapped her up in his arms in a little blanket and pinned her up with English pins, carried her and rocked her and, stopping work, would hurry to her as soon as he heard her cry. His first question on awakening or returning home was, "How's Sonya? Healthy? Did she sleep well? Eat?" Fyodor Mikhailovich would sit for hours on end at her bed, singing her songs or talking with her. In fact, by the third month he was convinced that she recognized him. Here is what he wrote to Maikov on May 18/May 29, 1868: "This little, three-month-old being, so helpless, so tiny - for me was already a face and a character. She had begun to recognize me, and love me, and she would smile when I came near. When I would sing her songs in my funny voice, she loved to listen to them. She did not cry and make a face when I kissed her. She stopped crying when I came near." But it was not to be for long that we

month he was convinced that she recognized him. Here is what he wrote to Maikov on May 18/May 29, 1868: "This little, three-month-old being, so helpless, so tiny - for me was already a face and a character. She had begun to recognize me, and love me, and she would smile when I came near. When I would sing her songs in my funny voice, she loved to listen to them. She did not cry and make a face when I kissed her. She stopped crying when I came near." But it was not to be for long that we would enjoy this cloudless happiness. In the first days of May the weather was glorious, and we, on the insistent advice of the doctor, would take our precious baby to the Jardin des Anglais, where she would sleep two or three hours in her carriage. On one fateful day during one of these walks, the weather changed suddenly, and a wind (bise) came up, and evidently our girl caught cold, because that same night she began to run a fever and developed a cough. We immediately consulted the best pediatrician in Geneva, and he saw her every day, assuring us that she would recover. Even just three hours before she died, he said that she was noticeably better. Despite his reassurances, Fyodor Mikhailovich could not concentrate on anything, and hardly left the side of her cradle. We both were in a horrible state of anxiety, and our gloomy forebodings proved justified: on the afternoon of May 12/24, our beloved Sonya died. It is beyond me to describe the despair that took hold of us when we saw our dear daughter dead. While I was deeply shaken and saddened by her death, I was terribly afraid for my unhappy husband: his despair was stormy, he sobbed and cried like a woman standing in front of the cold body of his beloved child, and covered her pale little face and hands with burning kisses. Such stormy despair I have never ever seen. It seemed to both of us that we would not survive our grief. For two days we went together, not parting for a moment, around to various institutions to get the permission to bury our little baby, together we ordered everything for her burial, together we dressed her in a white velvet dress, together we put her in a small white coffin lined with velvet, and we cried, cried without restraint. It was horrible to look at Fyodor Mikhailovich, in the week of Sonya's illness he had grown so thin. On the third day we took our little treasure to the Russian church for services, and from there to the cemetery in Plainpalais, where she was buried in a separate section for children. After several days her grave was planted around with cypress trees and in their midst a white marble cross was placed. Every day my husband and I would go to the cemetery, take flowers, and cry. It

white coffin lined with velvet, and we cried, cried without restraint. It was horrible to look at Fyodor Mikhailovich, in the week of Sonya's illness he had grown so thin. On the third day we took our little treasure to the Russian church for services, and from there to the cemetery in Plainpalais, where she was buried in a separate section for children. After several days her grave was planted around with cypress trees and in their midst a white marble cross was placed. Every day my husband and I would go to the cemetery, take flowers, and cry. It was just too painful for us to part with our priceless baby, so genuinely and deeply had we come to love her, so many hopes and dreams were tied with her very existence. 3. Letters Isolated from, homeland, friends, family, and his reading public, Dostoevsky relied heavily on his correspondence during the period he was writing The Idiot. The letters below have been selected for the insights they provide into the writing of The Idiot and the life of the man who wrote it. The translations and notes are from volumes 2 and 3 of David Lowe's edition of the Complete Letters of Dostoevsky (Ann Arbor, Mich.: Ardis, 1989 -90). The letter numbers in parentheses are the same as those used in the Russian edition of Dostoevsky's collected works. Lowe's notes are included, with slight alterations. AUGUST 16/28, 1867: APOLLON MAIKOV (NO. 317) This letter to Dostoevsky's close friend Apollon Maikov provides an excellent picture of Dostoevsky's state of mind as he settled down in Geneva to write The Idiot. It also describes the circumstances and conditions of the Dostoevskys' "exile" from Russia, Dostoevsky's feelings about his second wife, and his ruminations over the fate of Russia and contemporary ideological trends. It gives an entertaining account of his meeting with Turgenev. Like many of Dostoevsky's letters, it details his desperate finances and ends with a plea for a loan. For what a long time I've been silent and not answered your dear letter, dear and unforgettable friend Apollon Nikolaevich. 1 I call you unforgettable friend and feel in my heart that the title is correct: you and I go back so far and are so used to each other that life, which has sometimes separated and even pulled us apart, not only has failed in fact to pull us apart, but may even have brought us together permanently. If you write that you have rather felt my absence, then how much more have I yours. In addition to the conviction about the similarity and agreement in our views and feelings, which has been confirmed in me daily, consider as well that having lost you, I have landed, in addition, in an alien land, where not only is there not a Russian face, Russian books, or Russian thoughts and cares to be found, but not even a friendly face! 2 Really, I can't even understand how a Russian abroad, if only

but may even have brought us together permanently. If you write that you have rather felt my absence, then how much more have I yours. In addition to the conviction about the similarity and agreement in our views and feelings, which has been confirmed in me daily, consider as well that having lost you, I have landed, in addition, in an alien land, where not only is there not a Russian face, Russian books, or Russian thoughts and cares to be found, but not even a friendly face! 2 Really, I can't even understand how a Russian abroad, if only he has feelings and sense, can fail to notice this and feel it painfully. Perhaps those faces are in fact friendly for themselves, but to us it seems that they aren't for us. It really is that way! And how can one spend one's life abroad? Without one's native land it's suffering, honest to God! To go even for half a year, even for a year, is all right. But to go the way I have, without knowing and without foreseeing when I'll return, is very bad and distressing. The idea is distressing. And I need Russia, need it for my writing and work (not to mention the rest of life), and how badly I need it! It's just like a fish out of water; you lose your strength and means. In general we'll have a talk about this. I need to talk to you about a lot of things and ask your advice and help. You are the only person with whom I can speak from here. NB. By the way, read this letter off on your own and don't tell anyone about me who doesn't need to know. You'll see for yourself. One more word: why haven't I written to you in such a long time? I don't have the energy to answer you about that in detail. I felt myself to be too unstable and was waiting for the slightest settled way of life in order to begin a correspondence with you. I rely on you, on you alone. Write me more often; don't abandon me, my friend! And I'll be writing you very often and regularly now. Let's start up a constant correspondence, please! That will take the place of Russia for me and will give me energy. I'll tell you about these four months tant bien que mal [so -so] and frankly. You know how I left and for what reasons. The two main reasons: (1) to save not only my health, but even my life. The attacks had begun to repeat themselves every week, and clearly sensing and recognizing that nervous and cerebral disorder was unbearable. My reason really was coming unhinged - that's the truth. I sensed that, and the derangement of my nerves sometimes led to moments of frenzy. The second reason is my circumstances: the creditors could not wait any longer, and at the time I left, Latkin and Pechatkin had

bien que mal [so -so] and frankly. You know how I left and for what reasons. The two main reasons: (1) to save not only my health, but even my life. The attacks had begun to repeat themselves every week, and clearly sensing and recognizing that nervous and cerebral disorder was unbearable. My reason really was coming unhinged - that's the truth. I sensed that, and the derangement of my nerves sometimes led to moments of frenzy. The second reason is my circumstances: the creditors could not wait any longer, and at the time I left, Latkin and Pechatkin had already initiated proceedings against me for recovery of their money, 3 and I just barely avoided being seized. Let's suppose (and I'm not saying this for the sake of ornament and not for the sake of effect) debtors' prison, on the one hand, would have been even useful for me: the reality, the material, a second House of the Dead, in a word, there would have been enough material for four or five thousand rubles, but after all, I had just gotten married and, in addition, would I have survived that stifling summer in Tarasov's building? 4 --- Page 56 --- That made an insoluble question. If it were impossible for me to write in Tarasov's building, because of increased attacks, what would I pay off my debts with? And a terrible burden had accumulated. I set off, but I left back then with death in my heart: I didn't believe in abroad, that is, I believed that the psychic influence of abroad would be very bad: alone, without material, with a young person who with naive joy strove to share my peripatetic life with me; but after all, I saw that there was a great deal of inexperience and first ardor in that naive joy, and that disturbed me and worried me. I was afraid that Anna Gri -goryevna 5 would be bored with me. And after all, we really have been alone together until now. I wasn't relying on myself: I have a morbid personality, and I foresaw that she would be tormented to death with me. (NB. True, Anna Grigoryevna has turned out to be stronger and deeper than I knew her to be and imagined, and in many cases she has simply been a guardian angel for me; but at the same time there is much of the child and the twenty -year-old in her, which is wonderful and naturally essential, but to which I doubt that I have the energy and capacity to respond. All of that occurred to me during the departure, and although, I repeat, Anna Grigoryevna has turned out to be both stronger and better than I thought, I'm still not at ease yet.) Finally, our limited means worried me: we had left with quite small means and had borrowed an advance of three (!) thousand from Katkov. True, I planned to get down to work right away after leaving for abroad.

of the child and the twenty -year-old in her, which is wonderful and naturally essential, but to which I doubt that I have the energy and capacity to respond. All of that occurred to me during the departure, and although, I repeat, Anna Grigoryevna has turned out to be both stronger and better than I thought, I'm still not at ease yet.) Finally, our limited means worried me: we had left with quite small means and had borrowed an advance of three (!) thousand from Katkov. True, I planned to get down to work right away after leaving for abroad. What has turned out to be the case? I have not done anything or hardly anything yet and am only beginning to do serious, finished work now. True, I'm still in doubt with regard to my not having done anything; but lots of various things have been felt sharply and thought up; but there is still only a little down in black and white, 6 and after all, what's in black and white is in fact what's final; that's the only thing they pay for. After quickly leaving boring Berlin (where I stayed for one day, where the boring Germans nonetheless had time to unsettle my nerves to the point of fury and where I went to a Russian bathhouse), we moved on to Dresden, rented an apartment, and settled in for a while. A strange impression resulted; right away the question occurred to me of why I was in Dresden, precisely in Dresden, and not somewhere in another place, and for what precisely had it been worth dropping everything in one place and moving to another. The answer was clear (health, debts, and so on), but what was also bad was that I sensed very clearly that no matter where I live now, it turns out to be all the same, in Dresden or anywhere, anywhere abroad, everywhere a loner. I had wanted to set immediately to work and sensed that I absolutely couldn't work, that the impression was absolutely the wrong one. What did I do? Led a vegetable existence. I read, wrote a bit, suffered from ennui, and then from the heat. The days passed monotonously. Anya and I regularly took walks after dinner in the Grand Garden, listened to cheap music, then read, and then went to sleep. In Anna Grigoryevna's personality there has turned out to be absolute antiquarianism (and that's even pleasant and amusing for me). For her, for example, it's a thoroughly satisfying undertaking to go examine some silly town hall, make notes about it, describe it (which she does with her own stenographic marks and has filled up seven tablets), but more than anything else she has been taken and struck by the Gallery, and I was very glad for that, because too many impressions were revived in her soul for her to get bored. She went to the Gallery every day. How much have she and I talked about and discussed

to be absolute antiquarianism (and that's even pleasant and amusing for me). For her, for example, it's a thoroughly satisfying undertaking to go examine some silly town hall, make notes about it, describe it (which she does with her own stenographic marks and has filled up seven tablets), but more than anything else she has been taken and struck by the Gallery, and I was very glad for that, because too many impressions were revived in her soul for her to get bored. She went to the Gallery every day. How much have she and I talked about and discussed all our friends, Petersburgers, Muscovites, you and Anna Ivanovna; 7 it was rather sad. I'm not describing my thoughts to you. Many impressions have accumulated. I read Russian newspapers and unburdened my heart. I sensed in myself at last that enough material had accumulated for a whole piece about Russia's relations to Europe and about the Russian upper stratum. 8 But what's the point of talking about that! The Germans upset my nerves, and the life of our Russian upper stratum and its faith in Europe and civilization did too. The incident in Paris shook me horribly.9 The Paris lawyers who shouted "Vive la Pologne" [Long live Poland] are fine ones too. Ugh, what vileness, and most importantly - stupidity and conventionalism. I also became even more convinced of my former idea that it's even rather to our advantage that Europe doesn't know us and knows us in such a foul way. And the details of the trial of that shitty Beryozovsky!10 How much vile conventionalism; but most importantly, most importantly - how is it they haven't run out of things to say, how is it they're still stuck in the same spot, the very same spot! From here Russia also seems more distinct to people like me. The extraordinary fact of the stability and unexpected maturity of the Russian people in encountering all our reforms (if only the legal one alone) and at the same time the news about the merchant of the first guild of Orenburg Province flogged by the chief of police. 11 One thing can be felt: that the Russian people, thanks to its benefactor and his reforms, 12 has finally been put little by little into such a position that it is being forced to become accustomed to efficiency and self -observation, and that's the whole point. Honest to God, because of the great break and the reforms, the time now is nearly more important than Peter the Great's. And how about the railroads? Let's have some to the south soon, as soon as possible; that's the whole point. By that time there will be correct judgment everywhere, and then what a great renewal! (All of that is being thought about, dreamed of, and wished for with all one's heart here.) Here, although I've hardly seen anyone, it's impossible not to run into people by accident. whole point. Honest to God, because of the great break and the reforms, the time now is nearly more important than Peter the Great's. And how about the railroads? Let's have some to the south soon, as soon as possible; that's the whole point. By that time there will be correct judgment everywhere, and then what a great renewal! (All of that is being thought about, dreamed of, and wished for with all one's heart here.) Here, although I've hardly seen anyone, it's impossible not to run into people by accident. In Germany I ran into a Russian who lives abroad permanently, goes to Russia every year for three weeks or so to receive his income, and returns to Germany again, where his wife and children are; they've all become Germanized. By the way, I asked him why exactly he had become an expatriate. He answered literally (and with irritated impudence): "There is civilization here, while we have barbarism. In addition, there aren't any nationalities here; I rode the train yesterday and couldn't tell a Frenchman from an Englishman or from a German." "So in your opinion, that means that's progress?" "But of course." "But you know, that's absolutely not so. A Frenchman is above all a Frenchman, and an Englishman an Englishman, and being themselves is their highest goal. Moreover, that is in fact their strength." "Absolutely untrue. Civilization ought to level everything, and we won't be happy until we forget that we're Russians, and everyone looks like everyone else. We shouldn't listen to Katkov, after all!"13 "You don't like Katkov?" "He's a scoundrel." "Why?" "Because he doesn't like Poles." "And do you read his journal?" "No, I never read it." I'm conveying that conversation literally. This person belongs to the young progressives, although he seems to steer clear of everyone. They all turn into peevish and squeamish spitzes abroad. Finally, in Dresden ennui wore Anna Grigoryevna and me out. And most importantly, the following facts presented themselves: (1) according to the letters that Pasha 14 forwarded me (he in fact only wrote me once), it turned out that my creditors had instituted proceedings against me for recovery of the money (that means that I cannot return to Russian until the money is paid). (2) My wife felt herself to be pregnant (that is, please, between us. Nine months will be up in February: that means there's all the more reason I cannot return.). (3) The question arose of what would happen to my people in Petersburg, Emilia Fyodorovna 15 and Pasha and certain others. Money, money, and I didn't have any! (4) If we were to spend the winter, then we should winter somewhere in the south. Moreover, I wanted to show Anna Grigoryevna at least something, to amuse her, to travel a bit with her. We decided to winter somewhere in Switzerland or in Italy. But no money. The money we'd borrowed had already been spent. I wrote to Katkov, described my situation, and asked for an

more reason I cannot return.). (3) The question arose of what would happen to my people in Petersburg, Emilia Fyodorovna 15 and Pasha and certain others. Money, money, and I didn't have any! (4) If we were to spend the winter, then we should winter somewhere in the south. Moreover, I wanted to show Anna Grigoryevna at least something, to amuse her, to travel a bit with her. We decided to winter somewhere in Switzerland or in Italy. But no money. The money we'd borrowed had already been spent. I wrote to Katkov, described my situation, and asked for an advance of another 500 rubles. And what do you think: it was sent! What a wonderful person he is! What a heart the man has! We set --- Page 57 --- off for Switzerland. But here I'll begin describing for you my vile and shameful deeds. Dear Apollon Nikolaevich, I sense that I could have considered you as my judge. You're a person and a citizen; you're a person with a heart, of which fact you have long since persuaded me; you're a model husband and father; finally, I have always valued your judgment. It's not painful for me to confess to you. But I'm writing this only for you, just you. Don't turn me over to the tongues of idle gossips. Passing not far from Baden, it occurred to me to drop in there. I was tormented by a tempting idea: of sacrificing 10 louis d'ors and maybe winning 2000 extra francs; after all, that is living expenses for four months, with everything, with the Petersburg people. Most awful of all is that previously I had had occasion to win sometimes. And worst of all is that my nature is vile and very passionate: everywhere and in everything I go to the last limit; I've been going over the line my whole life. The devil immediately played a prank on me: in some three days I won 4000 francs, with extraordinary ease. Now I'll depict for your how all of this presented itself to me: on the one hand, that easy win - in three days I had made four thousand francs out of a hundred. On the other hand, the debts, the legal proceedings, the spiritual alarm, the impossibility of returning to Russia. Finally, third and most important - the gambling itself. You know how that draws you in. No, I swear to you, it's not just self -interest, although above all I needed the money as money. Anna Grigoryevna implored me to be satisfied with the 4000 francs and leave immediately. But after all, such an easy and possible chance to fix everything! And examples? In addition to my own winnings you see people getting 20,000 or 30,000 francs daily. (You don't see losers.) What makes them sacred? I need money more then they do. I took a further risk and lost. I began losing the last of my money, getting feverishly irritated, and lost. I started

in. No, I swear to you, it's not just self -interest, although above all I needed the money as money. Anna Grigoryevna implored me to be satisfied with the 4000 francs and leave immediately. But after all, such an easy and possible chance to fix everything! And examples? In addition to my own winnings you see people getting 20,000 or 30,000 francs daily. (You don't see losers.) What makes them sacred? I need money more then they do. I took a further risk and lost. I began losing the last of my money, getting feverishly irritated, and lost. I started pawning my clothing. Anna Grigoryevna pawned everything of hers, her last things (What an angel! How she tried to comfort me, how she languished in thrice -cursed Baden, in our two rooms over a blacksmith shop, where we had moved.). Finally, it was enough, everything was lost. (Oh, how vile the Germans are at the same time. What usurers, scoundrels, and cheats they all are, every last one of them. The landlady for our apartment, realizing that for the time being, until we got money, we had no place to go, increased the price!) Finally, we had to save ourselves, to leave Baden. I wrote Katkov again, asked for 500 rubles again (without mentioning the circumstances, but the letter was from Baden, and he probably understood a thing or two). Well, sir, he sent it! He sent it! I have now taken a total advance of 4000 from The Russian Herald. But here's what's the matter: out of that 500, more than half went for the payment of the interest and the re -pawning of our furniture in Petersburg, which Anna Grigoryevna's mother did. At my request, the money from The R[ussian] Herald was addressed to her. Then 100 rubles went to pay off debts in Baden; we're still waiting for 50 rubles: Anna Grigoryevna's mother will send it (out of the same 500 rubles. That's the remainder that's not yet been received); and finally, about two hundred francs were left for the move to Geneva (Why to Geneva? How should I know? Isn't it all the same?). We moved to Geneva, rented a chambre garnie [furnished room] from two old women, and now, that is, on the fourth day, all the money we have left is 18 francs. Except for the 50 rubles that we expect any day from Anna Nikolavna, there is no prospect of receiving any money for a couple of months. But to finish with Baden, we suffered through seven weeks in that hell. At the very beginning, when I had just arrived in Baden, I met Goncharov at the train station. How embarrassed Ivan Alex-androvich was by me at first. That deputy counselor of state or counselor of state was also losing. Anna Nikolavna, there is no prospect of receiving any money for a couple of months. But to finish with Baden, we suffered through seven weeks in that hell. At the very beginning, when I had just arrived in Baden, I met Goncharov at the train station. How embarrassed Ivan Alex-androvich was by me at first. That deputy counselor of state or counselor of state was also losing. But since it turned out that it was impossible to hide, and that in addition I myself was gambling with very crude candor, he stopped hiding from me too. He gambled with a feverish ardor (for small stakes, silver), gambled for the 2 weeks that he stayed in Baden and, I think, lost a significant amount. But God grant him health, the nice man; when I had lost my shirt (and he had seen a lot of gold in my hands), he gave me a loan of 60 francs, at my request. He must have reproached me horribly for losing everything, and not half, as he had done. Goncharov kept talking to me about Turgenev, so that although I had put off going to see Turgenev, I finally ventured to pay him a visit. I went at 12:00 noon and found him having breakfast. I'll tell you candidly: even before that I disliked the man personally. Worst of all is that since 1867, since Wiesbaden, I have owed him 50 thalers (and haven't yet paid them back!). I also dislike the aristocratically farcical embrace of his with which he starts to kiss you but offers you his cheek. The horrible airs of a general; but most important, his book Smoke had put me out. He himself told me that the main idea, the main point of his book was contained in the phrase "If Russia disappeared, there would not be any loss or any agitation among mankind." He told me that was his fundamental conviction about Russia. I found him terribly put out by the failure of Smoke. And I confess that I did not know all the details of the failure. You had written me about Strakhov's article in Notes of the Fa[therland], but I didn't know that he had been given a lashing everywhere and that in Moscow, at a club, I think, people were collecting names in order to protest his Smoke. He told me that himself. I confess to you that I could never have imagined that one could expose the wounds to one's vanity as naively and awkwardly as Turgenev does. And those people boast of the fact, by the way, that they are atheists! He declared to me that he is an atheist through and through. But my God, deism gave us Christ, that is, such a lofty notion of man that it cannot be comprehended without reverence, and one cannot help believing that this ideal of humanity is everlasting! And what have they, the Turgenevs, Herzens, Utins, 16 and Chernyshevkys presented us with? Instead of the

you that I could never have imagined that one could expose the wounds to one's vanity as naively and awkwardly as Turgenev does. And those people boast of the fact, by the way, that they are atheists! He declared to me that he is an atheist through and through. But my God, deism gave us Christ, that is, such a lofty notion of man that it cannot be comprehended without reverence, and one cannot help believing that this ideal of humanity is everlasting! And what have they, the Turgenevs, Herzens, Utins, 16 and Chernyshevkys presented us with? Instead of the loftiest divine beauty, which they spit on, they are all so disgustingly selfish, so shamelessly irritable, flippantly proud, that it's simply incomprehensible what they're hoping for and who will follow them. He criticized Russia and Russians monstrously, horribly. But here's what I've noticed: all these trashy little liberals and progressives, primarily still of Belinsky's school, find their greatest pleasure and satisfaction in criticizing Russia. 17 The difference is that Chernyshevsky's followers simply criticize Russia and openly wish for its collapse (primarily for its collapse!). Those people, Belinsky's offspring, add that they love Russia. But meanwhile not only is everything of the slightest originality in Russia hateful to them, so that they deny it and immediately take enjoyment in turning it into a caricature, but that if one really were to present them finally with a fact that they could not overturn or ruin in a caricature, but to which they definitely would have to be reconciled, I think that they would be unhappy to the point of torture, to the point of pain, to the point of despair. (2) I noticed that Turgenev, for instance (just like everyone who has not been to Russia in a long time), are absolutely ignorant of the facts (although they do read newspapers) and have so crudely lost every bit of feeling for Russia, fail to understand such ordinary facts, which even our Russian nihilist no longer denies, but simply caricatures in his own way. By the way, Turgenev said that we ought to crawl before the Germans, that there is one path common to everyone and unavoidable - civilization, and that all attempts at Russia -nism and independence are swinishness and stupidity. He said that he was writing a long article against Russophiles and Slavophiles. 18 I advised him, for the sake of convenience, to order a telescope from Paris. "What for?" he asked. "It's far from here," I replied, "Train your telescope on Russia and examine us, because otherwise it really is hard to make us out." He got horribly angry. Seeing him so irritated, I really said to him with extraordinarily successful naivete: "But I really didn't expect that all this criticism of you and the failure of Smoke would irritate you so much; honest to God, it isn't worth it, forget about it all." "But I'm not at all irritated. What do you mean?" he said, turning red. I interrupted

Really, the common people here are much worse and more dishonest then ours, and what is sillier, there's no doubt of that. Well, here you go on talking about civilization; well, what has civilization done for them and what can they boast of so very much before us?" He turned pale (literally; I'm not exaggerating a bit, not a bit!) and said to me: "In talking like that you offend me personally. You should know that I have settled in here permanently, that I consider myself a German, not a Russian, and I'm proud of that!" I replied: "Although I have read Smoke and have been speaking with you now for a whole hour, I couldn't at all have expected that you would say that, and therefore please forgive me for having offended you." Then we parted quite politely, and I vowed to myself never again to set foot at Turgenev's. On the following day Turgenev, at exactly 10 o'clock in the morning, came by to see me and left the landlords his calling card, to have it given to me. But since I myself had told him the day before that I could not receive him earlier than twelve o 'clock and that we slept until eleven, I took his arrival at 10 o'clock in the morning as a clear hint that he didn't want to meet me and had paid a visit to me at 10 o'clock precisely so that I would realize that. During the whole seven weeks I met him one time only, at the train station. We looked at each other, but neither he nor I wished to exchange greetings. But honest to God, I can't help it; he offended me too much with his convictions. one time only, at the train station. We looked at each other, but neither he nor I wished to exchange greetings.
