Entirely yours, Fyodor Dostoevsky JANUARY 1/13, l868: SOFIA IVANOVA (NO. 332) The addressee of this letter was Dostoevsky's beloved niece Sonya, to whom, as he informs her here, he dedicated The Idiot. In this letter, Dostoevsky records his intent in The Idiot to "depict a positively beautiful person. " Further, aside from attesting Dostoevsky's warm feelings for his sister's family, and especially for the addressee, this letter shows that as he wrote The Idiot, he was pondering the "woman question " in general as well as the choices faced by his niece in particular. My dear, priceless friend Sonechka, in spite of your insistent request to write you, I've been silent. And meanwhile, in addition to the fact that I experienced a strong and special need to talk to you, if for no other reason than that I definitely ought to have replied to a point in your letter 29 and replied right away, as soon as possible - I should have replied. Tell me: how could it have occurred to you, dear and eternal friend, that I left Moscow after becoming angry with you and didn't offer you my hand! But could that have happened? Of course, my memory is weak, and I don't remember details, but I assert positively tliat none of that could have happened, and that it just seemed that way to you. In the first place, there could not have been any reason; I know that as well as I do that twice two is four; and secondly, and most importantly, could I really break off with my friends so easily? So that's how you know

you, dear and eternal friend, that I left Moscow after becoming angry with you and didn't offer you my hand! But could that have happened? Of course, my memory is weak, and I don't remember details, but I assert positively tliat none of that could have happened, and that it just seemed that way to you. In the first place, there could not have been any reason; I know that as well as I do that twice two is four; and secondly, and most importantly, could I really break off with my friends so easily? So that's how you know me, my dear! How painful it was for me to read that. You ought to have realized, Sonya, how I appreciate and respect you, and how I value your heart. I've met few people in my life like you. You'll ask how, for what reasons I've become so attached to you. (You'll ask if you don't believe me.) But my dear, it's awfully hard to answer these questions; I remember you as practically a little girl, but I didn't begin peering into you and recognizing in you a rare, special person and a rare, wonderful heart until four years ago or so, and most importantly, I came to know you the winter that the late Marya Dmitrievna died. 30 Do you remember when I came to see you after a whole month of my illness, when I hadn't seen you for a very long time? I love you all, and you especially. I love Mashenka, 31 for example, for her charm, grace, naïveté, charming manner; but I only very recently came to know the seriousness of her heart (oh, you are all talented and marked by God) -but I'm especially attached to you, and the attachment is based on a special impression that is difficult to dissect and explain. I like your restraint, your inborn and elevated feeling of your own worth, and I like the consciousness of that feeling. (Oh, don't ever betray it in any way; take the straight path, without compromises in life. Fortify your good feelings in yourself, because you need to fortify everything, and you need only make a compromise with your honor and conscience once in order to have a weak spot remain in your soul, so that just as soon as something difficult, but on the other hand, advantageous, presents itself in your life, you'll immediately retreat before the difficult and head toward the advantageous. I'm not stating a commonplace now; what I'm saying is something that afflicts me myself; and I spoke of a weak spot perhaps out of personal experience. I may love in you precisely what leaves much to be desired in me.) I especially love in you that firm edifice of honor, view, and convictions, which, of course, is absolutely natural and not yet much realized by you, because you couldn't even have recognized all of it, by reason of your extraordinary youth. I also love your mind, which

life, you'll immediately retreat before the difficult and head toward the advantageous. I'm not stating a commonplace now; what I'm saying is something that afflicts me myself; and I spoke of a weak spot perhaps out of personal experience. I may love in you precisely what leaves much to be desired in me.) I especially love in you that firm edifice of honor, view, and convictions, which, of course, is absolutely natural and not yet much realized by you, because you couldn't even have recognized all of it, by reason of your extraordinary youth. I also love your mind, which is calm and discriminates clearly and distinctly, and sees truly. My friend, I agree with you about everything you've been writing me in your letters, but that I should ever agree about your accusation - that even the slightest fluctuation in my friendship for your has occurred - never! It's simply that perhaps everything should be explained through some minor matter, as some irritability of the moment in my vile character - but even that could never have been directed at you personally, but at someone else. Don't ever wound me with such accusations. But for the fact that I didn't answer for so long, in spite of your request to write you soon, I give you my word of honor that I'll send you a letter a month regularly. In my letter to Alexander Pavlovich and Verochka I explained, as much as I could, the reason for my silence. I was in such a bad psychic mood and under such tension the whole time that I felt the need to lock myself up inside myself and grieve on my own. It would have been depressing for me to take up a letter, and besides, what would I have written? About my melancholy? (It would surely have shown through in my letter.) But that's bad material. And besides, my situation was bothersome. My entire fate depends on my work. On top of that, I have taken an advance of around 4500 rubles from The Russian Herald, and I gave them my word of honor, and repeated it the whole year long in every letter to the journal, that there would be a novel. And then, almost just before sending the novel off to the journal, I had to throw it out, because it had ceased to please me. (And if it had ceased to please, I couldn't have done a good job of finishing it.) I destroyed much of what I had written. Meanwhile, both the repayment of my debt, and my daily life, as well as the whole future, were contained in the novel. Then, about three weeks ago (18 December, New Style), I tackled another novel32 and began working day and night. The idea of the novel is an old and favorite one of mine, but such a hard one that for a long time I didn't dare to take it up, and if I have taken it

it had ceased to please, I couldn't have done a good job of finishing it.) I destroyed much of what I had written. Meanwhile, both the repayment of my debt, and my daily life, as well as the whole future, were contained in the novel. Then, about three weeks ago (18 December, New Style), I tackled another novel32 and began working day and night. The idea of the novel is an old and favorite one of mine, but such a hard one that for a long time I didn't dare to take it up, and if I have taken it up now, then absolutely because I was in a nearly desperate situation. The main idea of the novel is to portray a positively beautiful person. There's nothing more difficult than that in the whole world, and especially now. All the writers, and not just ours, but even all the European ones, who ever undertook the depiction of a positively beautiful person, always had to pass. Because it's a measureless task. The beautiful is an ideal, and the ideal - both ours and that of civilized Europe - is far from having been achieved. There's only one positively beautiful person in the world - Christ, so that the appearance of this measurelessly, infinitely beautiful person is in fact of course an infinite miracle. (The whole Gospel of John is in this sense; he finds the whole miracle in the incarnation alone, in the appearance of the beautiful alone.) But I've gone too far. I'll just mention that of the beautiful people in Christian literature Don Quixote stands as the most complete. But he is only beautiful because he's ridiculous at the same time. Dickens' Pickwick (an infinitely weaker idea than Don Quixote, but still an enormous one) is also ridiculous, and effective in fact because of that. Compassion appears for the beautiful that is mocked and does not know its own value, and therefore, sympathy appears in the reader too. That arousal of compassion is in fact the secret of humor. Jean Valjean 33 is also a strong attempt, but he arouses sympathy by virtue of his misfortune and society's injustice to him. I have nothing like that, absolutely nothing, and therefore I'm terribly afraid that it will be an absolute failure. Certain details, perhaps, will be decent. I'm afraid it will be boring. The novel is long. I wrote the whole first part in twenty-three days and sent it off a few days ago. It will definitely not be striking. Of course, that's just the introduction, and it's good that nothing yet has been compromised; but hardly anything has been explained, hardly anything laid out yet. My only wish is that it arouse at least some curiosity in the reader, so that he'll take up the second part. I'll finish the second, which I'm beginning tomorrow, in a month (I've in fact worked like this my whole life).34 I think it will be stronger and better than the first. Wish

My dear friend, how I would wish that the novel turn out to be at least somewhat worthy of the dedication. In any case, I'm no judge of myself, especially not when so much in the heat of the moment as now. My health is absolutely satisfactory, and I can bear difficult work, although a difficult time is ahead for me, because of Anna Gri -goryevna's delicate condition. 35 I'll put in another four months or so at work, and then I dream of moving on to Italy. Solitude is essential --- Page 60 --- for me. I'm distressed about Fedya 36 and about Pasha. 37 I'm also writing to Fedya with this mail. It's very depressing for me abroad, however; I want terribly to go to Russia. Anna Grigoryevna and I are all alone. My life: I get up late, fuel the fireplace (it's terribly cold), drink coffee, then get down to work. Then at four o'clock I go have dinner at a restaurant and dine for two francs with wine. Anna Grigoryevna prefers to dine at home. Then I go to a café, drink coffee, and read The Moscow News and The Voice and reread them to the last letter. Then I walk through the streets a half an hour, for exercise, and then home and to work. Then I again fuel the fireplace, we drink tea, and again to work. Anna Grigoryevna says that she's terribly happy. Geneva is boring, gloomy, a stupid Protestant city, with a vile climate, but so much the better for work. Don't grieve, my friend, that's the main thing, and the main thing is not to hurry, not to worry very much; everything will come in its own time and will come fine, all on its own. There is an infinite number of chances in life; worrying too much means losing time. I wish you energy and firmness of character; I'm certain of them, however. My dear, look after your education and don't neglect even a profession, but the main thing is not to rush; you are still very young; everything will come in its own time, but you should know that the woman question, and especially that of the Russian woman, will definitely make several great and wonderful strides even within your lifetime. I'm not speaking of our precocious ladies - you know how I view them.40 But a few days ago I read in the papers that a former friend of mine, Nadezhda Suslova (Apol -linaria Suslova's sister), passed the examination for doctor of medicine at the University of Zurich and defended her dissertation brilliantly. She's a very young woman; she's twenty -three now, a rare personality, noble, honest, lofty! I had planned to write you a lot, too much, but here I've covered over so many pages and not written even one -tenth of it. Until the next letter, my friend! I would like to have a talk with you about one very interesting subject concerning you. 41 Don't forget me, and write. Take care of your health most of all. Embrace Masenka one more time. Kiss her too, if she'll allow a kiss (from me); kiss Vitya45 for me too, as well as the much praised Lyolya.46 Then everyone, and give your brother Alexander Alexandrovich 47 a firm handshake from me. MARCH 21 -22/aPRIL 2-3, l868: APOLLON MAIKOV (NO. 341) This letter to Maikov shows Dostoevsky in his new role of very proud and joyful father of a nezvborn daughter and in his habitual role of concerned and frustrated stepfather to Pasha Isaev and brother -in-law to Mikhail Dostoevsky's widow, Emilia. He expresses optimistic views about Russia's future and his perpetual concern about his progress with the novel and its success with readers. Dear and kind friend Apollon Nikolaevich, first, thank you very much, dear fellow, for carrying out all my errands, which turn out to be very troublesome and for which you have had to run around so much. Forgive me for tormenting you, but really, you're the only person I can rely on (which does not at all constitute a reason for tormenting you). Second, thank you for the greeting, congratulations, and best wishes for the three of us. You are right, my kind friend, you have described this feeling of being a father from nature and you have taken your wonderful words from nature: everything is absolutely right. I have been feeling, for almost a month now, terribly much that is new and that until now has been unfamiliar to me, from exactly that moment that I first saw my Sonya,48 up to that moment when, through joint efforts, we had just bathed her in a basin. Yes, an angelic soul has in fact flown in to be with us. But I won't describe my feelings to you. They are growing and developing with every day. Here's what, my dear fellow, the last time, when I wrote you in such alarm, I forgot (!) to write to you what Anya 49 and I had agreed on while still in Dresden last year (and what she reproached me horribly about for having forgotten now) - is that you're the godfather for my Sonya. Dear fellow, please agree! We've had it decided for almost ten months. If you refuse, that will be a misfortune for Sonya. Even her first godfather refused! But you won't refuse, my friend. I'll add that this won't cause even the slightest trouble for you, and that you'll become a godfather for us is so much the better. The godmother is Anna Nikolavna50 - has she told you? Please tell me your answer soon - because we need it for the christening. 51 It's been a month now, and she's still

- is that you're the godfather for my Sonya. Dear fellow, please agree! We've had it decided for almost ten months. If you refuse, that will be a misfortune for Sonya. Even her first godfather refused! But you won't refuse, my friend. I'll add that this won't cause even the slightest trouble for you, and that you'll become a godfather for us is so much the better. The godmother is Anna Nikolavna50 - has she told you? Please tell me your answer soon - because we need it for the christening. 51 It's been a month now, and she's still not christened! (Does that happen in Russia?) But your goddaughter (I'm sure that she's your goddaughter) - I'm telling you - is very cute - in spite of the fact that she looks impossibly, even ridiculously, like me. Even to the point of strangeness. I wouldn't believe it if I hadn't seen it. The child is only just a month old, but she already even has absolutely my facial expression, my whole face, including the wrinkles on the brow - she lies there and looks as though she's writing a novel! I won't even mention the features. Her brow resembles mine even to the point of strangeness. From that, of course, it should follow that she's not especially pretty (because I'm a good -looking man only in Anna Grigoryevna's eyes - and that's serious, I'll tell you!). But you, yourself an artist, know perfectly well that it's possible to resemble even an unattractive face and at the same time be very nice itself. Anna Grigoryevna begs you very much to be the godfather. She loves you and Anna Ivanovna 52 terribly much and respects you infinitely. You are too great a prophet: you have been prophesying for me that I have new cares now and will become an egoist, and that, unfortunately, because of impossibility, has come true quite otherwise: just imagine: I have not gotten a single line written this whole month! My God, what I'm doing to Katkov, to my promises, word of honor, obligations! I was unbelievably glad when, following my notification that I would be late, because of the birth, The Russian Herald printed at the end of the first part of the novel that the continuation would be in the April issue rather than the one for March. But unfortunately, I have no more than twenty days left for the April one, too (having been terribly late), and not a line has been written! I'm writing to Katkov tomorrow and apologizing, but really, my apologies are worthless to them. And nonetheless I have to be in time for the April issue, even though it is late. And meanwhile, in addition to everything else, my whole existence (financial means) depends on them. A truly desperate situation. But what can be done: the whole month passed in extraordinary worries, troubles, and cares. I sometimes didn't sleep for nights on end, and not just because of psychic uneasiness,

twenty days left for the April one, too (having been terribly late), and not a line has been written! I'm writing to Katkov tomorrow and apologizing, but really, my apologies are worthless to them. And nonetheless I have to be in time for the April issue, even though it is late. And meanwhile, in addition to everything else, my whole existence (financial means) depends on them. A truly desperate situation. But what can be done: the whole month passed in extraordinary worries, troubles, and cares. I sometimes didn't sleep for nights on end, and not just because of psychic uneasiness, but because that really was necessary. And for falling sickness that's terrible. My nerves are now on edge to the highest degree. We had a hideously vile March here - with snow and freezing temperatures, almost like in Petersburg. Anna Grigoryevna was horribly out of sorts physically. (Don't tell Anna Nikolavna that for anything, because she'll imagine God only knows what. It's just that it's taking Anya a long time to recover, and, in addition, she's feeding the baby herself.) There's little milk, of course. We use a bottle as well. The baby, however, is fine. (Knock on wood!) Anya is finally beginning to go out for walks. We've had amazing sunny days for three days now, and the greenery is already starting to sprout. I can hardly come to from all this. Money is also a terrible worry. Three hundred rubles was sent to us. That's 1025 francs after the exchange. But we already have hardly anything. Expenses have increased, we had to pay off our earlier debts, redeem our pawned things, and exactly three weeks from today there are large expenditures in store, in connection with changing our apartment (we're being thrown out of this one for the baby's crying), and, in addition, there are some payments ahead - it's horrible. And in addition we have to live, beginning today, for at least two months or so until there's the hope of getting some more from The Russian Herald. But in any case I can't get anything from The Russian Herald without sending them the second part - and when will I get it written? Surely not again in eighteen days, like --- Page 61 --- what was published in the January issue? Your handling of the money was very good. And painful as it is for me to trouble you, send me the 25 rubles you have left here, to Geneva, immediately, if possible. That's how urgent the need is! (NB. Just put a 25-ruble note in a letter, register it, so that it doesn't get lost at the post office, and send it here, to my address. They change our Russian paper money here as well. But here's what: we now have new twenty -five ruble notes, I think. I'm afraid they don't know them here yet. So it's better to send old bills.) I'm very glad that you gave Pasha 53 50 rubles instead of

I'm very glad that you gave Pasha 53 50 rubles instead of 25. That's all right. I'm awfully glad that he's working.54 Dear fellow, check up on him occasionally, at least from time to time! If I write him, I'll write that after finding out that you had given him 25 rubles as a loan, I have already repaid you. But here's what: can it really be that Pasha isn't going to write me anything and isn't going to congratulate me on the birth of Sonya? Other people have congratulated me: you, Strakhov, from Moscow, Anna Grigoryevna's friends from Petersburg, 55 but not only has there not been anything from Pasha even now, but I haven't even received an answer to a letter of mine that I wrote him about a month and a half ago, addressing it care of you. (Did you get it? For some reason you don't mention it.) And he could answer that one. By the way, there's also something extremely important: after all, I still don't know whether he went to Moscow or not. Did he go to see Katkov? That's terribly important for me to know.56 Just imagine - I sent Katkov a long letter of apology exclusively apropos of that! I need to know extremely badly. Can't you please find out, dear fellow? (I informed Emilia Fyodorovna officially, solemnly, that I have a new daughter - nothing, no response from her! And earlier, too, there was no reply from her to an extremely important question of mine regarding their apartment and the landlord, Alonkin. That even surprises me. After all, that's even outrageously rude!) Regarding a will and other such advice of yours I have always, always been of your opinion myself. 57 But my friend, my sincere and devoted friend (perhaps the only one!), why do you consider me so kind and generous? No, my friend, no, I'm not particularly kind, and that saddens me. But poor Marya Dmitrievna 58 left me Pasha on her deathbed. How can I completely abandon him? (But you don't even advise that.) At least from time to time I have to help him, the more so as I sincerely love him; after all, I raised him in my home from the age of ten! He's the same as a son. He and I lived together. And at such a young age, and alone, to leave him on his own - is that really possible? At least sometimes, poor

my friend, no, I'm not particularly kind, and that saddens me. But poor Marya Dmitrievna 58 left me Pasha on her deathbed. How can I completely abandon him? (But you don't even advise that.) At least from time to time I have to help him, the more so as I sincerely love him; after all, I raised him in my home from the age of ten! He's the same as a son. He and I lived together. And at such a young age, and alone, to leave him on his own - is that really possible? At least sometimes, poor as I may be, I have to help him. He's a horribly frivolous person, that's true; but after all, at his age I may have been even worse than he (I remember). He needs to be supported just now. If a good, kind impression is left in his heart now, it will be of use to him in his further development. And that he's in service and himself now earning - I'm terribly, terribly glad about that - let him work a bit. And you I hug and kiss fraternally for having gone to see Razin and taking care of him there. 5 As for Em[ilia] Fyodorovna, after all, the point again is my deceased brother Misha. And really, you don't know what that man was for me my whole life, from the first moment of consciousness on! No, you don't know that! Fedya 60 is my godson and also a young man who earns his keep through hard work. Here too, if it's at all possible, I need to support him sometimes (because he's a young man, he can't have everything around his neck, it's too heavy, sir). But why do you, my dear fellow, make yourself out to be such a practical person and an egoist? Aren't you the one who gave me a loan of 200 rubles and who with the death of my brother Misha and the collapse of the journal lost almost 2000 rubles? But I was wrong to start up about all this. I consider your advice, in any case, absolutely correct. And actually, with regard to me, there is a proverb: don't boast when you're headed into battle. I write that apropos of needing to help them, and so on. But how do I know what's going to happen to me? No matter how hideous, no matter how vile life abroad has become for me, I sometimes think with horror about what will happen to my health when I manage to return to Petersburg. After all, if I have attacks here, what about there? My mental capacities, memory, for instance, are absolutely lost. Everything that you write about Russia, and most importantly, your mood (the rosy color), makes me very happy. It's absolutely true that there's no point in paying attention to various individual cases; 61 as long as there is an integral whole and an impetus and a goal for that whole,

how hideous, no matter how vile life abroad has become for me, I sometimes think with horror about what will happen to my health when I manage to return to Petersburg. After all, if I have attacks here, what about there? My mental capacities, memory, for instance, are absolutely lost. Everything that you write about Russia, and most importantly, your mood (the rosy color), makes me very happy. It's absolutely true that there's no point in paying attention to various individual cases; 61 as long as there is an integral whole and an impetus and a goal for that whole, and everything else cannot be any other way in view of such an enormous regeneration as under our present great sovereign. 62 My friend, you view things absolutely the same way I do, and you have finally said what I even said aloud three years ago, at the time I was still publishing the journal, and what people misunderstood me about, namely: that our constitution is in fact the reciprocal love of the monarch for the people and of the people for the monarch. 63 Yes, our state's basis of love, rather than of conquest (which the Slavophiles were first to discover, I think), is a very great idea, on which much will be built. We'll tell this idea to Europe, which doesn't understand anything at all about it. Our mis -fortunate class of clever fellows, 64 who have lost touch with the soil, had to end up like that, alas. They'll die like that; you can't remake them! (Turgenev!) But the new generation - that's where you have to look. (A classical education could help very much. What is Katkov's lyceum?) 65 Here abroad I have definitely become an absolute monarchist for Russia. If anyone among us has done anything, then, of course, it is only he (and not just because of that, but simply because he's tsar, loved by the Russian people, and personally because he's tsar. Our people has always given and continues to give its love to every tsar and ultimately believes only in him. For the people that's a mystery, a priesthood, an unction). The Westernizers don't understand anything about this, and they, boasting of their basis in facts, have overlooked the main and greatest fact of our history. I like your idea about Peter's pan -Slavic significance. I've heard that idea for the first rime in my life, and it's absolutely true. 66 But here's what: I read The Voice here. Terribly sad facts are sometimes presented in it. For instance, about the disarray of our railroads (the newly -built ones), about zemstvo affairs, about the sad state of the colonies. 67 It's a horrible misfortune that we still have few people, doers. There are talkers, but when it comes down to business, you can count them on the fingers of one hand. Of course I don't mean people who get things done in lofty matters, but simply the petty bureaucrats of whom we

rime in my life, and it's absolutely true. 66 But here's what: I read The Voice here. Terribly sad facts are sometimes presented in it. For instance, about the disarray of our railroads (the newly -built ones), about zemstvo affairs, about the sad state of the colonies. 67 It's a horrible misfortune that we still have few people, doers. There are talkers, but when it comes down to business, you can count them on the fingers of one hand. Of course I don't mean people who get things done in lofty matters, but simply the petty bureaucrats of whom we need many, and who are lacking. Let's assume that there have been enough people for judges and jurors. But on the railroads? And several other places too. A terrible confrontation of new people and new needs with the old order. I'm not talking about bringing their ideas to life: there are lots of freethinkers, but no Russians. Most importantly, the consciousness of a Russian person in oneself - that's what's needed. But how openness helps the tsar and all Russians - oh, Lord, it's inimical, Westernizing. I would terribly much like our political railroads to get established quickly (Smolensk, Kiev, but quickly, and new rifles quickly too!).68 Why has Napoleon increased his forces and risked a thing so unpleasant for his people, at such a critical moment for himself? The D[evil] only knows. But it won't lead to anything good for Europe. (I somehow believe that terribly much.) It's bad if they drag us in too. If only it can be after a couple of years. And not just Napoleon. Besides Napoleon the future is frightening, and we need to prepare for it. Turkey is hanging by a thread, Austria is in too abnormal a situation 69 (I'm just analyzing the elements and not judging about anything), the cursed Western proletarian question that has become terribly developed (which is hardly mentioned in daily politics!), and lastly, Napoleon is an old man and in poor health. He won't live long. During that time he'll create even more failures, and the Bona- partes will sicken France even more - and what will happen then? Russia definitely must prepare, and quickly, because this may happen terribly soon. How glad I am that the heir apparent has shown himself to Russia in such a good and majestic way and that Russia is giving such evidence of its hopes for him and its love of him. 70 Yes, let there be even only half the love that there is for his father, and that would be enough. But may God grant that our Alexander live another forty years. He has done for Russia practically more than all his predecessors taken together. 71 But the most important thing is that he is so loved. All Russian movement now, all regeneration, is based on that support and only on it. Oh, my friend, how I would like to return, how sickening my life here is. An awful

evidence of its hopes for him and its love of him. 70 Yes, let there be even only half the love that there is for his father, and that would be enough. But may God grant that our Alexander live another forty years. He has done for Russia practically more than all his predecessors taken together. 71 But the most important thing is that he is so loved. All Russian movement now, all regeneration, is based on that support and only on it. Oh, my friend, how I would like to return, how sickening my life here is. An awful life. And most importantly, work is not going well. If only I could do a decent job of finishing the novel, 72 how good that would be! That's the basis of my whole future. Anna Grigoryevna doesn't pine and says sincerely that she's happy. But I'm miserable. I don't go anywhere and don't see anyone. But even if there were friends I don't think I'd go visit. I've gone completely to seed, but the work still isn't moving. I only leave the apartment for two hours a day, at five o'clock, and go to a café to read Russian papers. I don't know anyone here and am glad for that. It's disgusting even to meet our clever fellows. Oh, poor people, oh, worthless people, oh, trash, swollen from --- Page 62 --- vanity, oh, shit! It's disgusting! 73 H[erzen] and I met by chance on the street, talked ten minutes in a hostilely polite tone, with little gibes, and went our separate ways. No, sir, I won't go, sir. How they, oh, how they have fallen behind, to what an extent they fail to understand anything. And they've swollen up, how they've swollen up! I read here thirstily the newspaper announcements of the appearance of issues of journals and the titles. Strange names and contents of issues, for instance, Notes of the Father [land], yes, rags instead of banners, that's true! Dear fellow, don't give them anything, wait a bit. But the question of where to publish obviously even troubles you. Don't worry, my friend. I'm writing you in a hurry right now, otherwise I'd have a talk with you. I have an idea for you, but it requires special exposition, in a whole letter, and I don't have the time right now. I'll write soon. This idea occurred to me apropos of your "Sofia Alexeevna." And believe me, it's serious, don't laugh. You'll see for yourself what sort of an idea it is! I'll lay it out. It's not a novel and not a narrative poem. But it's so needed, will be so essential, and will be so original and new and with such an essential, Russian tendency that you yourself will gasp! I'll lay out the program for you. It's too bad that it will be in writing, rather than in a live conversation. You'll be able to become famous with this, and most important, it will have

me apropos of your "Sofia Alexeevna." And believe me, it's serious, don't laugh. You'll see for yourself what sort of an idea it is! I'll lay it out. It's not a novel and not a narrative poem. But it's so needed, will be so essential, and will be so original and new and with such an essential, Russian tendency that you yourself will gasp! I'll lay out the program for you. It's too bad that it will be in writing, rather than in a live conversation. You'll be able to become famous with this, and most important, it will have to be issued in a separate book, after printing several excerpts beforehand, and the book will have to sell a huge number of copies. So have you finished Revelations? 74 I had imagined that you had abandoned it. There's no way of avoiding the ecclesiastical censorship, of course, and it's impossible any other way, but if you have been translating it absolutely accurately, they'll permit it. I received a letter from Strakhov. It made me happy. I want to write him soon, but since he didn't write me his address (he forgot!), I'll answer him via you. And I'll ask you to deliver it. Dear fellow, write me more often. You wouldn't believe what your letters mean for me! It's already April 3, local style, and the 25 th is the last deadline (minimum!) for sending off the novel, and I don't have a single line, not a single line written! Lord, what's going to happen to me! Well, goodbye. I kiss you and embrace you, Anya sends her regards, and we both send our regards to Anna Ivanovna. Entirely yours, Fyodor Dostoevsky P.S. Please write me everything you hear (if only you hear anything) about The Idiot. I need that, need it, definitely need it! Please! The finale of the second part, about which you wrote, is the same that is published at the end of the first part. I was relying on that! I still believe in the absolute truth of Nastasya Filippovna's character, however. By the way: many little things at the end of the first part are taken from nature, and certain characters are simply portraits, for instance, General Ivolgin, Kolya. But your judgment may in fact be very accurate. 75 MAY 18/30 MAY, l868: APOLLON MAIKOV (NO. 346) A heartbroken Dostoevsky writes of his baby daughter Sonya's death. His question "But where is Sonya?" will be raised in different guises in The Idiot and other subsequent works. Thank you for your letter, my dear Apollon Nikolaevich, and for the fact that after getting angry at me, you didn't cease corresponding with me. I always, at the bottom of my heart, was certain that Apollon Maikov wouldn't do that. My Sonya 76 has died; we buried her three days ago. Two hours before her death I didn't know that she would die. Three hours before her death the doctor said she was better and that she

daughter Sonya's death. His question "But where is Sonya?" will be raised in different guises in The Idiot and other subsequent works. Thank you for your letter, my dear Apollon Nikolaevich, and for the fact that after getting angry at me, you didn't cease corresponding with me. I always, at the bottom of my heart, was certain that Apollon Maikov wouldn't do that. My Sonya 76 has died; we buried her three days ago. Two hours before her death I didn't know that she would die. Three hours before her death the doctor said she was better and that she would live. She was sick for only a week; she died of pneumonia. Oh, Apollon Nikolaevich, my love for my first child may have been ridiculous; I may have spoken of it ridiculously in many letters of mine to people congratulating me. The only person ridiculous for them was me alone, but I'm not afraid to write to you, to you. That little three-month-old creature, so poor, so tiny, was already a personality and a character for me. She was beginning to know and love me and to smile when I approached. When I sang songs to her with my funny voice she loved to listen to them. She didn't cry and frown when I kissed her; she would stop crying when I approached. And now people tell me by way of consolation that I'll have more children. But where is Sonya? Where is that little person for whom I state boldly that I would accept crucifixion if only she could be alive? But let's stop this; my wife is crying. We'll finally take our leave of our little grave the day after tomorrow and go away somewhere. Anna Nikolavna 77 is with us: she arrived just a week before her death. The last two weeks, beginning with the onset of Sonya's illness, I haven't been able to work. I sent an apology to Katkov again, and again only three chapters will appear in The Russian Herald, in the May issue. 78 But I hope that now I'll be working day and night, without a break, and beginning with the June issue the novel will come out at least decently. Thank you for agreeing to be the godfather. She was christened eight days before her death. I know, my friend, that I'm very guilty before you for not yet having returned the money borrowed from you and, in addition, for giving part of the money that I only recently received from Katkov to Emilia Fyodorovna79 and Pasha 80 and not yet to you, when you must now be in very great need. 81 But you can't repair anything with regrets, and therefore I'll tell you straight out everything I can tell you for certain: at the present moment I can't give back anything; I myself have almost nothing, and in leaving Geneva, I even pawned my clothes and my wife's (I'm only telling you this). I don't dare ask Katkov

returned the money borrowed from you and, in addition, for giving part of the money that I only recently received from Katkov to Emilia Fyodorovna79 and Pasha 80 and not yet to you, when you must now be in very great need. 81 But you can't repair anything with regrets, and therefore I'll tell you straight out everything I can tell you for certain: at the present moment I can't give back anything; I myself have almost nothing, and in leaving Geneva, I even pawned my clothes and my wife's (I'm only telling you this). I don't dare ask Katkov for anything right now, since I've been deceiving him for three months now. But in a month and a half and at the most in two (that's for certain) - I'll ask Katkov to send you 200 rubles from me. That's for certain. As for the fact that I have not yet thought of you, that's unfair, honest to God. It bothered me a lot; but what can I tell you? I can't tell you anything. Just remember one thing, Apollon Nikolaevich, that when I borrowed that 200 rubles from you back then, even then I was borrowing almost half for them, for my relatives, and 75 of the 200 rubles went to them via you. I think that's how it was, as far as I recall. I thank you very much for having saved me then, and I very much appreciate your tact with me until now, in spite of the fact that your situation was a difficult one, about which I have now learned. By the way, one great request: don't pass on the news about my Sonya dying to any of my relatives if you see them. At least I wouldn't like them to know about this for a while, including, of course, Pasha. I think that not only will none of them feel bad about my child, but perhaps even the contrary, and the thought of that alone enrages me. In what way is that poor creature to blame before them? Let them hate me, let them laugh at me and at my love - I don't care. Forgive me for Pasha's bothering you so. I don't understand what's going to become of him. What's he leading up to? 82 The two positions that he had could have made him honest and independent. What a tendency, what views, what ideas, what bragging! It's typical. But once again, on the other hand, how can I leave him like that? After all, a little bit more and such ideas will result in a Gorsky or a Raskolnikov.83 After all, they're all madmen and fools. I don't know what's to become of him. I just pray to God for him. Also by the way: he has not replied a thing to my letter three months ago. I couldn't have written him more gently. He to such an extent regards me exclusively as someone obliged to send him money that

what views, what ideas, what bragging! It's typical. But once again, on the other hand, how can I leave him like that? After all, a little bit more and such ideas will result in a Gorsky or a Raskolnikov.83 After all, they're all madmen and fools. I don't know what's to become of him. I just pray to God for him. Also by the way: he has not replied a thing to my letter three months ago. I couldn't have written him more gently. He to such an extent regards me exclusively as someone obliged to send him money that he didn't even write me a couple of lines, even if only as a postscript to someone's letter, to congratulate me when Sonya was born. I would rather he not learn of her death. I have also learned that he has in his hands some extraordinarily important letters that have come for me this year. (One of them from my earlier friend Krukovskaya.) How could I get them forwarded to me here. That's very, very important for me. 84 He may have other letters as well. Good-bye, my friend. I'll try to write you from the new place. Montreux, which you write about, is one of the most expensive and fashionable places in all of Europe. I'll look for a little village near Vevey. Your translation of Revelations is magnificent, but it's too bad that's it isn't all of it. I read it yesterday. Entirely yours, Fyodor Dostoevsky My wife thanks you for everything and asks you to keep Sonya's little icon for her. 85 NOTES BY DAVID LOWE --- Page 63 --- 1. IV SELECT BIBLIOGRAPHY The list below contains works relevant to The Idiot in particular. For a full bibliography of recent works on Dostoevsky, see: W. J. Leath -erbarrow, Fedor Dostoevsky: A Reference Guide (Boston: G. K. Hall, 1990) and the bibliographical listings in the "Current Bibliography" section of each issue of Dostoevsky Studies {Journal of the International Dostoevsky Society). Editions, Translations, Sources COLLECTED WORKS Dostoevskii, F. M. Polnoe sobranie sochinenii v tridtsati tomakh (Complete collection of works in thirty volumes). 30 vols. Edited by V. G. Bazanov et al. Leningrad: Nauka, 1972-90. This definitive edition of Dostoevsky's works and letters includes superb detailed commentary about all the works. THE IDIOT (TRANSLATIONS) The Idiot was first translated into English in 1887 by Frederick Whish -aw. Constance Garnett translated the novel in 1913. (She translated all of Dostoevsky's works; for decades Dostoevsky was known to the English -speaking world largely through her translations.) Her translation of The Idiot is still in print (New York: Bantam). Of the translations available, probably the best to use are those of Henry and Olga Carlisle (New York: New American Library, 1969) and of Alan Myers (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1992), both because of the quality of the translations and because of the explanatory notes. The translation by David Magarshack (London: Penguin, 1955) lacks notes. NOTEBOOKS For help in deciphering these notebooks and in understanding their relationship to the finished novel, readers should consult Robin Feuer Miller's article in this volume. The Russian text of the notebooks in which Dostoevsky recorded plans and ideas for The Idiot and helpful commentary on these notebooks are found in volume 9 of his collected works. These notebooks have also been published in the following editions: Dostoevskii, F. M. Iz arkhiva F. M. Dostoevskogo. Idiot: neizdannye materialy. P. N. Sakulina i N. F. Bel'chikova. Miller, Robin Feuer. Dostoevsky and "The Idiot. " Author, Narrator, and Reader. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1981. The most comprehensive and detailed reading of the novel, illuminating the process whereby the reader of this novel is challenged to "choose" whom and what to believe. It examines the split of author and narrator (and, consequently, of author's reader and narrator's reader) and shows that this narrative technique stems from Dostoevsky's view that the truth of this novel must be told "slant." It includes discussion of the notebooks of the novel (excerpted for this volume) and of subtexts (especially the Gothic novel and Rousseau) of the inserted narratives. ---------. Bethea is Vilas Professor of Slavic Languages at the University of Wisconsin, Madison. In addition to The Shape of the Apocalypse in Modern Russian Fiction (chapter 1 of --- Page 71 --- which appears here), he has written Kodasevich: His Life and Art and Joseph Brodsky and the Creation of Exile, and edited a book on Pushkin ( Pushkin Today). His newest book is Realizing Metaphors: Alexander Pushkin and the Life of the Poet. Liza Knapp, associate professor of Russian literature at the University of California, Berkeley, is the editor and translator of Dostoevsky as Reformer: The Petrashevsky Case and the author of The Annihilation of Inertia: Dostoevsky and Metaphysics. Robin Feuer Miller, professor of Russian and comparative literature and dean of arts and sciences at Brandeis University, is the author of Dostoevsky and "The Idiot": Author, Narrator, and Reader (from which the study here is taken) and The Brothers Karamazov: Worlds of the Novel, and the editor of Critical Esssays on Dostoevsky. With Donna Orwin, she edited Kathryn Feuer's Tolstoy and the Genesis of "War and Peace," and with Malcolm Jones, the Cambridge Companion to the Classic Russian Novel. Robin Feuer Miller, professor of Russian and comparative literature and dean of arts and sciences at Brandeis University, is the author of Dostoevsky and "The Idiot": Author, Narrator, and Reader (from which the study here is taken) and The Brothers Karamazov: Worlds of the Novel, and the editor of Critical Esssays on Dostoevsky. With Donna Orwin, she edited Kathryn Feuer's Tolstoy and the Genesis of "War and Peace," and with Malcolm Jones, the Cambridge Companion to the Classic Russian Novel. A new hook, Dostoevsky: Transformations and Conversions, is forthcoming. Nina Pelikan Straus is professor of literature at Purchase College, State University of New York. Her Dostoevsky and the Woman Question: Rereadings at the End of a Century, is excerpted here. She has also written on Kundera, Kafka, and Conrad from a feminist perspective, as well as introductions to works of Mann and Hardy.
